label: 0–3 months
title: Newborn
note: Every baby is unique. These milestones are general guides — premature babies may reach them later.

milestones.text: Lifts head briefly when on tummy

guide.try: Do tummy time for 1–2 minutes several times a day on a firm surface. Get down to her eye level — she’ll try harder to lift her head to see your face.
guide.watch: Head wobbles up even briefly; she strains toward your face during tummy time.
guide.see: Head shows no lifting at all by 3 months, or neck feels unusually floppy.

guide.try: Lay her on her back and gently cycle her limbs in slow, smooth motions. Bath time is ideal — warm water makes movement easier.
guide.watch: Both sides move with similar frequency and strength during awake time.
guide.see: One side consistently moves much less than the other, or limbs seem very stiff or very floppy.

guide.try: Keep mittens off during awake time so she can discover her hands. Gently guide her hands toward her face so she experiences the sensation.
guide.watch: Hands drift toward her mouth or face during relaxed, alert moments.
guide.see: No hand-to-face movement by 3 months alongside other motor concerns.

relatedActivities.name: Gentle tummy time
relatedActivities.tagline: The foundation of every movement they will ever make.

relatedActivities.name: Head control practice
relatedActivities.tagline: Holding the head steady is step one of every milestone after it.

relatedActivities.name: Discovering their own hands
relatedActivities.tagline: The most fascinating toy is attached to their own arm.

relatedActivities.name: Reach for the dangling toy
relatedActivities.tagline: The first swipe at a toy is the first draft of every reach that follows.

guide.try: Respond consistently and promptly to every cry in these early months. Reliable responses build trust and, over time, actually reduce overall crying.
guide.watch: Different cry patterns begin to emerge for hunger, discomfort, and tiredness.
guide.see: Baby rarely cries even when hungry or uncomfortable, or the cry is consistently very weak or high-pitched.

guide.try: When she makes a sound, pause and mimic it back — this serve-and-return teaches her communication is two-way. Talk and sing constantly.
guide.watch: Soft vowel-like sounds (ooh, aah) appear during calm, alert periods.
guide.see: No vocalisation other than crying by 3 months.

guide.try: Test gently with a hand clap or rattle outside her line of sight when she is calm and awake. A clear startle confirms her hearing is active.
guide.watch: Eyes widen, arms briefly extend, or she startles visibly at sudden sounds.
guide.see: No startle response to loud sounds by 1 month warrants a hearing check.

relatedActivities.name: Talk through what you're doing
relatedActivities.tagline: The words don't matter yet. Your voice does.

relatedActivities.name: Sing one song, every day
relatedActivities.tagline: The same song, the same voice, again and again.

relatedActivities.name: Sound location game
relatedActivities.tagline: Hearing where something comes from is a skill, not an instinct.

relatedActivities.name: Serve and return
relatedActivities.tagline: The oldest conversation in the world — with no words on either side.

relatedActivities.name: Read aloud — anything
relatedActivities.tagline: It is never too early. The story doesn't matter. Your voice does.

relatedActivities.name: Cooing conversations
relatedActivities.tagline: Ooh and ahh back — you're teaching turn-taking.

guide.try: Hold your face 20–30 cm away — that is the clearest focal distance for a newborn. Make slow, exaggerated expressions and give her time to lock on.
guide.watch: Eyes fix on your face and stay there; she may go still as she concentrates.
guide.see: No interest in faces by 6 weeks, especially combined with no eye contact.

guide.try: Smile broadly and slowly at her during calm awake time. The social smile typically emerges at 6–8 weeks. Increase face-to-face time if it hasn’t appeared.
guide.watch: A genuine smile — corners of mouth lift, cheeks engage — in response to your face or voice.
guide.see: No social smile by 3 months.

milestones.text: Calms when held or spoken to

guide.try: Try skin-to-skin holding, a familiar soft song, or placing her against your left chest so she hears your heartbeat. Consistency matters more than technique.
guide.watch: Crying reduces or stops when you pick her up, make eye contact, or speak calmly.
guide.see: Baby is consistently impossible to soothe — worth discussing to rule out reflux or other causes.

relatedActivities.name: Skin-to-skin hold
relatedActivities.tagline: Your chest is their first home outside the womb.

relatedActivities.name: One-to-one time with each caregiver
relatedActivities.tagline: Every familiar face is a safe face they are storing.

relatedActivities.name: Chase the smile
relatedActivities.tagline: The first smile meant just for you.

relatedActivities.name: Silly faces and the first laugh
relatedActivities.tagline: The funniest thing in the world is your face doing something unexpected.

guide.try: Hold a high-contrast card or your face 25 cm away and move it slowly left to right. Give her time to lock on before moving.
guide.watch: Eyes track across the midline smoothly from one side to the other by 2–3 months.
guide.see: No visual tracking at all by 2 months, or significant eye crossing after 3 months.

guide.try: Speak to her from outside her line of sight and watch for her to turn her head or still in response. Use her name often in a warm tone.
guide.watch: She quiets, turns toward you, or shows alerting behaviour when she hears your voice.
guide.see: No response to voices or sounds by 3 months.

guide.try: Place black-and-white cards with stripes, checkers, or a bull’s-eye in her line of sight during alert periods. Swap them weekly to maintain novelty.
guide.watch: She gazes at the pattern and slows her movement to concentrate on it.
guide.see: No visual interest in anything in her environment by 2 months.

relatedActivities.name: Face-to-face gazing
relatedActivities.tagline: You are the most interesting thing they have ever seen.

relatedActivities.name: Gentle baby massage
relatedActivities.tagline: Touch is the first language. Speak it slowly.

relatedActivities.name: High-contrast visual exploration
relatedActivities.tagline: Black and white are the colours of a newborn's world.

relatedActivities.name: Texture exploration
relatedActivities.tagline: Their hands are learning what the world feels like.

activityIntro.headline: The fourth trimester — settling into the world together.
activityIntro.desc: The first quarter is about regulation, not stimulation: warmth, touch, and the sound of your voice while your baby's nervous system catches up to the world outside the womb. By month three, alert windows lengthen and the first real smiles and coos arrive. Use the month tabs below — the first month is broken down week by week, since change is fastest here.
activityIntro.note: Growth spurts around 3 weeks, 6 weeks, and 3 months often bring fussier days. More holding, not more stimulation, is usually the answer.

activities.name: Skin-to-skin hold
activities.tagline: Your chest is their first home outside the womb.
activities.why: Skin-to-skin contact (kangaroo care) regulates your baby's body temperature, heart rate, and cortisol levels more effectively than an incubator. It also triggers oxytocin release in both parent and baby — the neurological foundation of attachment. This is not passive cuddling. It is active brain development.
steps: Undress your baby to their nappy. Open your shirt or hold them against your bare chest.
Cover both of you with a light blanket. Make sure their head is turned to one side, airway clear.
Let them rest. You don't need to do anything. Your heartbeat, warmth, and scent are the activity.
Any caregiver can do this — partner, grandparent. The baby benefits from each person's unique scent and rhythm.
activities.parentNote: "You might feel self-conscious at first, or unsure if you're doing it right. You are. There is no wrong way to hold your baby on your chest. If they settle, you're doing it right."
watchFor: Baby rooting or turning head — they may be hungry, pause and feed
Their breathing becoming slow and even — this means they feel safe
Stiffening or arching — they may be overstimulated, try a different position

activities.name: Talk through what you're doing
activities.tagline: The words don't matter yet. Your voice does.
activities.why: A newborn cannot understand words, but they can distinguish their caregiver's voice from all others within 24 hours of birth. Hearing your voice activates Broca's area — the language centre of the brain — even at this stage. Every word you say is laying neural pathways they will use for the rest of their life.
steps: During any routine — nappy change, feeding, dressing — narrate it quietly. "Now I'm putting your left arm in. There. That's warmer."
Use a slightly higher pitch than normal — instinctive "parentese" is actually optimal for infant language development.
Pause after you speak. Even at this age, babies are learning the rhythm of conversation — speak, pause, wait.
activities.parentNote: "You may feel odd talking to someone who cannot respond. That feeling passes. You're not performing — you're building something. The conversation starts long before the reply comes."
watchFor: Eyes widening or moving toward your voice — early attention
Mouth movements after you speak — proto-conversation beginning
Calming when you start talking — voice recognition in action

activities.name: Face-to-face gazing
activities.tagline: You are the most interesting thing they have ever seen.
activities.why: A newborn's visual range is exactly 20–30 cm — the distance from your breast to your face during feeding. This is not a coincidence. The human face is the first complex visual stimulus the brain is wired to process. Extended eye contact activates mirror neurons and is the earliest form of social learning.
steps: Hold your baby at 20–30 cm from your face. This is roughly cradled-in-arms distance.
Make eye contact and stay still. Let them find your face — don't move toward them.
Try a slow, exaggerated expression — a wide smile, raised eyebrows, a surprised "O" mouth. Wait. Watch.
When they look away, they are processing. Wait for them to look back before continuing.
activities.parentNote: "Looking away is not rejection. It is their nervous system saying 'I need a moment to process all of this.' It is the first time they are managing their own stimulation. Let them."
watchFor: Eyes tracking your face as you move slowly side to side — visual tracking beginning
A fleeting smile — likely a reflex at this age, but practice for the social smile at 6 weeks
Hiccups or yawning after — signs of gentle overstimulation, end the session

activities.name: Gentle tummy time
activities.tagline: The foundation of every movement they will ever make.
activities.why: Tummy time activates and strengthens the neck, shoulder, and core muscles that underpin every motor milestone — rolling, sitting, crawling, walking. It also builds the vestibular (balance) system. Even 3 minutes a day in week one makes a measurable difference by month three. It is hard at first. That is normal.
steps: Wait 20–30 minutes after a feed. Place baby face-down on your bare chest first — this is the gentlest introduction.
Alternatively, lay them on a firm, flat surface. Get down to their level — your face in front of theirs is the best motivation to lift their head.
Start with 1–2 minutes. Increase by 1 minute each week. Never leave them unsupervised on their tummy.
If they cry, pick them up. Try again tomorrow. Crying during tummy time is effort, not distress — but trust your instinct on the difference.
activities.parentNote: "Most newborns dislike tummy time. This does not mean you are doing it wrong. It means their muscles are working hard. Your calm presence beside them — not rescuing immediately — teaches them that difficult things are survivable."
watchFor: Head lifting briefly off the surface — even 1 second counts in week one
Arms pushing down — early upper body strength developing
Face turning to one side — if always the same side, mention to your pediatrician (possible torticollis)

activities.name: Sing one song, every day
activities.tagline: The same song, the same voice, again and again.
activities.why: Repetition is the mechanism of infant learning. A song heard daily becomes a neural pattern — a predictable, safe structure in an unpredictable world. Music activates more regions of the brain simultaneously than almost any other stimulus. It also regulates your nervous system while you sing, which your baby feels.
steps: Pick one song — any song. A lullaby, a Bollywood song you love, something from your childhood. The song does not matter. Consistency does.
Sing it at the same moment each day — before the bath, during the last feed, at nappy changes.
Sing it even if you feel you can't sing. The quality of your voice is irrelevant. Its familiarity is everything.
activities.parentNote: "You may feel silly singing to someone whose eyes are closed. Sing anyway. By week three, they will stir when they hear it. By week six, they will look for your face when it starts. You will remember this moment."
watchFor: Stilling when the song starts — auditory recognition forming
Eyes opening or searching — connecting sound to source
Siblings singing it too — this becomes a family ritual without you planning it

activities.name: Gentle baby massage
activities.tagline: Touch is the first language. Speak it slowly.
activities.why: Massage stimulates the vagus nerve — the primary pathway of the parasympathetic nervous system — reducing cortisol and increasing serotonin and dopamine. Research from Tiffany Field at the Touch Research Institute shows that massaged infants gain weight faster, sleep better, and show lower stress markers. It also teaches your baby the boundaries of their own body.
steps: Use a small amount of cold-pressed coconut or sunflower oil. Warm it in your palms first.
Begin with the legs — least sensitive area. Long, slow strokes from hip to foot. Always toward the heart.
Move to the arms, then the back (baby on tummy). Keep pressure light — fingertip pressure, not hand pressure.
Watch their face throughout. If they look away or fuss, pause. Ask permission with your eyes before continuing.
activities.parentNote: "Ask permission — even from a baby who can't answer. Say 'Can I massage your legs now?' and pause. This is not theatre. It is the earliest lesson in bodily autonomy. You are teaching consent before they have words for it."
watchFor: Limbs relaxing and uncurling — nervous system responding positively
Eyes going heavy — deep relaxation, a good sign
Persistent crying or stiffening — stop and comfort, try again another day

activities.name: High-contrast visual exploration
activities.tagline: Black and white are the colours of a newborn's world.
activities.why: A newborn's retina detects contrast more than colour — their colour vision develops over months. High-contrast black and white patterns activate the visual cortex strongly, building the neural architecture for pattern recognition, object permanence, and eventually reading. This is visual literacy beginning at two weeks.
steps: Draw or print simple high-contrast patterns — bold stripes, concentric circles, a simple face — on A4 paper.
Hold 20–30 cm from baby's face during an alert window.
Move it slowly left and right — watch their eyes track it. This is voluntary eye movement developing.
You don't need to buy anything. A printed sheet works. Your face in strong light is still the best stimulus.
activities.parentNote: "There is an entire industry selling black-and-white cards. A printed piece of paper does the same thing. Do not let the noise around parenting products obscure the simplicity of what your baby actually needs."
watchFor: Eyes widening and fixing on the pattern — visual attention focusing
Head turning to follow movement — early visual tracking
Eyes glazing or looking past the card — they've had enough, end the session

activities.name: Morning light walk
activities.tagline: Natural light is the first clock their body learns to read.
activities.why: A newborn's circadian rhythm — their internal day-night clock — is not yet set. Morning light exposure in the first weeks is the primary mechanism by which the brain sets this clock. Babies who get morning light in week 2–4 show significantly better sleep consolidation by 6–8 weeks. This activity benefits the parent's circadian rhythm too.
steps: Step outside for 10–20 minutes in the morning — ideally before 10am. Even in a carrier or pram.
Indirect morning light is enough — you don't need direct sun. An open balcony or shaded garden works.
If outdoors isn't possible, sit by a window with natural light. The lux levels still help.
This is also for you. Step outside every day, even briefly. It is one of the most evidence-backed interventions for postnatal mood regulation.
activities.parentNote: "Get dressed and go outside. Not for long. Not for any particular reason. Just to remind your body that the world is still there, and that you are still in it. Your baby benefits from the light. You benefit from the movement and the sky."
watchFor: Baby becoming drowsy in fresh air — a good response
Your own mood lifting slightly — notice this and build on it
Over-squinting or distress in bright light — shade their face and reduce direct exposure

activities.name: Sound location game
activities.tagline: Hearing where something comes from is a skill, not an instinct.
activities.why: Auditory localisation — knowing where a sound comes from — requires the brain to triangulate timing differences between both ears. This develops rapidly in weeks 3–8. Practicing it actively accelerates spatial awareness, and also exercises the neck muscles as baby turns toward sound. This is auditory development and motor development in the same moment.
steps: Hold baby on their back. Make a soft sound — a gentle rattle, a click of your tongue, your voice — about 30 cm to their left.
Wait. Watch their eyes and head. Don't rush to their eyeline.
After 15–20 seconds, try the same sound from the right.
Your voice is the best instrument. "Where's Mama?" from the left, then from the right, then appearing in their eyeline. Simple. Delightful for them.
activities.parentNote: "The moment they turn their head toward your voice and find your face — you will feel something shift. That is recognition. That is them choosing you. Let that land."
watchFor: Eyes moving toward sound before head does — neural pathway firing
Head turning to find the source — auditory localisation developing
Consistent preference for one side — mention to pediatrician if always the same

activities.name: Texture exploration
activities.tagline: Their hands are learning what the world feels like.
activities.why: The hands have the highest concentration of sensory nerve endings in the body. In week 3, the palmar grasp reflex is still dominant — but deliberate texture exposure begins building the sensory maps in the parietal cortex that underpin fine motor control, object recognition, and eventually writing. This is your baby's first science experiment.
steps: During a nappy change or calm moment, gently open your baby's fist and stroke the palm with different textures — your fingertip, a soft muslin, a slightly rough cotton.
Name what you're using: "This is soft. This is the muslin." Language + sensation together.
Let them grip your finger. Hold still and let them feel the pressure of their own grip.
activities.parentNote: "You don't need sensory toys for this. Your hands, a muslin, a cotton t-shirt. Complexity is not what they need. Variation is. The real world is the toy."
watchFor: Fingers spreading or curling around the texture — active exploration beginning
Eyes moving to their own hands — early hand-eye connection forming
Withdrawal from a texture — respect this, try again another day

activities.name: One-to-one time with each caregiver
activities.tagline: Every familiar face is a safe face they are storing.
activities.why: Attachment theory is often discussed only in terms of the primary caregiver. But infants benefit from multiple secure attachments — each caregiver's unique voice, scent, rhythm, and face is being catalogued and stored as safe. A baby held by their father, their grandparent, their sibling is not being passed around. They are expanding their circle of safety.
steps: The non-primary caregiver holds baby for 10 minutes during an alert window — not just when the primary caregiver needs a break, but as a deliberate activity.
They talk, sing, or simply hold in skin-to-skin. Their voice, their smell, their heartbeat rate.
If a sibling is involved — sit them beside you, let them hold a finger, sing to the baby. Inclusion is the activity.
activities.parentNote: "For the non-primary caregiver: this is yours. Not a handover, not a break for someone else. The baby is learning who you are. Show them slowly, calmly, in your own way. There is no right way to be held by someone who loves you."
watchFor: Baby settling into a new caregiver's hold — trust transferring
Eyes searching for the new face — curiosity, not distress
Siblings becoming gentler over time — empathy developing through proximity

activities.name: Serve and return
activities.tagline: The oldest conversation in the world — with no words on either side.
activities.why: Harvard's Center on the Developing Child identifies "serve and return" interaction as the single most important experience for healthy brain development. When a baby coos or makes a face and a caregiver responds — and then waits — neural connections are built in the prefrontal cortex governing communication, thinking, and emotional regulation. This is how brains are built, one exchange at a time.
steps: Face your baby at 20–30 cm. Wait for them to make any sound or movement — a coo, a mouth movement, an eyebrow raise.
Mirror it back. Coo if they cooed. Raise your eyebrows if they did. The match doesn't need to be perfect — the response is what matters.
Then wait. A genuine pause — 5 to 10 seconds. Give them time to serve again.
Don't flood the exchange with words. Less input, more waiting. The pause is the activity.
activities.parentNote: "The hardest part of this is the waiting. We are conditioned to fill silences. But the pause is where your baby is processing — building the next move. When you wait, you are teaching them that their response matters. That is the beginning of confidence."
watchFor: A coo or mouth movement after your response — they served back
Eye contact held for longer than before — engagement deepening
The beginning of a smile that reaches their eyes — this is the one you will never forget

activities.name: Sensory walk — a new environment
activities.tagline: The world is a sensory classroom. You don't need to teach — just take them in.
activities.why: By week four, your baby's sensory processing is more sophisticated than it was at birth. New environments — different light, wind, ambient sound, movement — provide novel multi-sensory input that stimulates the brain's reticular activating system, the region responsible for attention and arousal regulation. Nature is the most complex and the most calibrated sensory environment that exists.
steps: Go somewhere with natural variation — a garden, a tree-lined street, a park. Not a mall.
Narrate as you go: "Can you feel that? That's wind. And that sound is a crow. And that's a leaf — it's moving."
Let them feel — a leaf against their palm, the temperature change in and out of shade, the different sounds of a busy and a quiet street.
This is for you too. Walk slowly. You are not getting somewhere. You are being somewhere.
activities.parentNote: "One month ago you brought someone home who had never seen a tree, heard a bird, or felt wind. Today you are showing them the world. That is an enormous thing, and it looks like an ordinary walk."
watchFor: Eyes widening at light through leaves — visual complexity engaging
Stilling at a new sound — auditory attention developing
Falling asleep quickly after — sensory integration happening in sleep

activities.name: Read aloud — anything
activities.tagline: It is never too early. The story doesn't matter. Your voice does.
activities.why: Reading aloud to a newborn is one of the highest-ROI activities in early development. It exposes them to a wider vocabulary range than spoken conversation alone, models the rhythm of written language, and most importantly — it is a daily ritual of closeness with your voice. Children who are read to from birth show measurably stronger language outcomes at ages 3, 5, and 7.
steps: Read anything. A children's book, a novel you're reading, a news article, a recipe. They cannot understand the words. They are learning that words have rhythm, that reading is a calm activity, that your voice sounds different when you read.
Hold the book so they might see the page, but don't worry if they don't look. Hearing is the activity.
5 minutes counts. Every day matters more than any single session.
activities.parentNote: "Read them your book. Read them the menu. Read them the label on the back of the shampoo bottle at 3am when that's all you have. It counts. It always counts."
watchFor: Settling when you start reading — rhythm recognition
Eyes moving to the page — visual curiosity beginning
You feeling calmer — reading aloud regulates your breathing too

activities.name: Chase the smile
activities.tagline: The first smile meant just for you.
activities.why: The social smile — a genuine grin in response to a face, not a reflex from gas or sleep — is the first sign your baby recognizes interaction itself as rewarding. It marks a shift from reflexive to intentional social behavior, an early milestone in emotional development. Responding warmly to it reinforces that connection is worth initiating.
steps: During an alert window, get close (20–30cm) and smile broadly, holding it for a few seconds.
Wait. Give them several seconds to organize a response before you change your expression.
When they smile, smile bigger back and narrate: "There it is! There's your smile!"
activities.parentNote: "After weeks of wondering if anything you do registers, this is the answer. It registers. It always did. Now you can see it on their face."
watchFor: Smile reaching the eyes, not just the mouth — a true social smile
Smiling more at familiar faces than strangers — recognition forming
No social smile at all by 8–10 weeks — worth mentioning at the next check-up

activities.name: Cooing conversations
activities.tagline: Ooh and ahh back — you're teaching turn-taking.
activities.why: Cooing — soft, drawn-out vowel sounds like "ooh" and "aah" — is the first stage of vocal experimentation, laying groundwork for babbling around month four. When you imitate the sound back, you reinforce vocal play and model the alternating rhythm of real conversation. This is speech and language therapy dressed up as play.
steps: When your baby coos, pause what you're doing and coo back, matching their pitch loosely.
Wait for them to respond again before making another sound.
Add simple words around the coos: "Ooh, are you telling me something? Tell me more."
activities.parentNote: "It will feel like nonsense. It isn't. You are their first conversation partner, and this is the first draft of every conversation they will ever have."
watchFor: Increasing variety of vowel sounds — vocal range expanding
Cooing more when you respond — reinforcement working
Sounds paired with kicking or arm movement — whole-body excitement

activities.name: Head control practice
activities.tagline: Holding the head steady is step one of every milestone after it.
activities.why: By 8 weeks, most babies can briefly hold their head in line with their body when pulled to sit, and lift it further during tummy time. Head control is the base layer of the motor pyramid — every later skill (sitting, crawling, walking) depends on the neck and core strength being built now.
steps: Continue daily tummy time, now working up to 3–5 minutes at a stretch, multiple times a day.
Try supported upright holds against your shoulder or chest, letting them practice lifting and turning their head.
Gentle "pull to sit" from lying down (hands supporting, not pulling by the arms) lets them feel their neck engage.
activities.parentNote: "You'll feel the difference in your arms before you see it — the head lag getting shorter, week by week. That's strength you helped build."
watchFor: Head steadier and more upright when held sitting — core strength emerging
Less head lag during pull-to-sit — a milestone worth noting
Persistent significant head lag past 3 months — flag to your pediatrician

activities.name: Discovering their own hands
activities.tagline: The most fascinating toy is attached to their own arm.
activities.why: Around month three, babies discover their hands are theirs — a foundational moment in body awareness (proprioception) and the earliest form of cause-and-effect learning: "I move, and something moves." This precedes intentional reaching and grasping by only a few weeks.
steps: Lay baby on their back, arms free of blankets or swaddle, during alert time.
Let them simply watch and swat at their own hands — resist the urge to hand them a toy right away.
Gently bring their hands together at midline and narrate: "Look, your hands found each other."
activities.parentNote: "There is nothing to teach here. Just give them the time and the freedom to notice. Discovery doesn't need your help — it needs your patience."
watchFor: Staring intently at their own moving hand — visual-motor connection forming
Hands meeting at the midline — a motor milestone in itself
Batting at nearby objects — early reaching beginning

activities.name: Silly faces and the first laugh
activities.tagline: The funniest thing in the world is your face doing something unexpected.
activities.why: Laughter typically emerges between 3–4 months, first triggered by surprise and exaggerated expressions rather than anything conceptually "funny." A first laugh is a sign of growing cognitive processing — your baby is comparing what they expect to what they see, and registering the mismatch as delightful.
steps: Try gentle surprise: a peekaboo behind your hands, a funny sound, a raspberry on their tummy.
Repeat whatever gets a reaction — babies love predictable surprises far more than novel ones at this age.
Watch their cues; if they turn away or fuss, they've had enough stimulation for now.
activities.parentNote: "The first laugh is one of those sounds you will try to describe to people for years and never quite manage. Record it if you can. You'll want it back."
watchFor: Gasping giggles building to a full laugh — a huge milestone
Anticipatory smiling before the "surprise" lands — they're learning the pattern
Overstimulation (turning away, fussing) — a natural stopping point

activities.name: Reach for the dangling toy
activities.tagline: The first swipe at a toy is the first draft of every reach that follows.
activities.why: Purposeful swiping and early reaching begin around 10–12 weeks, a precursor to the intentional grasping that dominates month four onward. This requires the brain to link what the eyes see with where the hand needs to go — a core visual-motor skill.
steps: Dangle a light, high-contrast toy or object about 20cm above their chest, within easy reach.
Hold it still and let them attempt to swipe or bat at it — resist moving it toward their hand.
Celebrate any contact, even accidental. "You got it! You reached it!"
activities.parentNote: "The swipes will be clumsy and mostly miss. That's the point. Missing and trying again is how the aim gets better."
watchFor: Both hands swiping toward the object — bilateral coordination developing
Brief grasping on contact — the grasp reflex being used intentionally
Strong preference for one hand this early — usually not meaningful, mention if persistent

label: 4–6 months
title: Early explorer
note: Tummy time is crucial now — aim for 30 min spread through the day to build neck and core strength.

guide.try: Continue tummy time daily and add supported sitting on your lap so she practises balancing her head against gravity.
guide.watch: Head stays upright without bobbing during supported sitting; lifts fully during tummy time.
guide.see: Head still very floppy with no control by 4 months.

guide.try: Place a favourite toy just out of reach during tummy time to give her a reason to shift her weight. Never leave her unattended on an elevated surface now.
guide.watch: She shifts her weight to one side and rotates — tummy to back usually comes first.
guide.see: No rolling in either direction by 6 months.

guide.try: Hold toys within easy reach and slightly off to one side. Dangle objects during tummy time — reaching while prone builds more muscle groups.
guide.watch: Intentional reach toward objects with the hand closing around them.
guide.see: No reaching or batting at objects by 5 months.

guide.try: Offer safe teething toys and varied textures. Mouthing is important developmental exploration — her mouth is her most sensitive sensory tool at this age.
guide.watch: Grasped objects reliably make their way to the mouth with clear intention.
guide.see: Not typically a red flag alone, but flag if combined with very weak grasp or no hand use.

relatedActivities.name: Reach and grasp play
relatedActivities.tagline: Crossing the midline is a brain milestone disguised as a toy grab.

relatedActivities.name: Rolling practice
relatedActivities.tagline: The first way they'll ever travel on their own.

guide.try: Imitate her sounds back to her then add one new consonant sound. The more varied language input she hears, the richer her output will become.
guide.watch: Consonant-vowel combinations (ba, da, ma, ga) appear alongside cooing.
guide.see: No babbling or consonant sounds by 6 months.

guide.try: Use her name consistently and pause after saying it to allow response time. Start with visual contact — let her see your face while you call her name.
guide.watch: She turns her head or looks toward you specifically when her name is called.
guide.see: No response to her own name by 6 months warrants a hearing check.

guide.try: Gentle tummy raspberries, peek-a-boo, and funny slow facial expressions are classic triggers. Find what she responds to most and repeat it often.
guide.watch: Belly laugh or sustained giggling during playful interaction with familiar people.
guide.see: No laughter or joyful vocalisations by 6 months.

guide.try: Ensure several consistent caregivers interact with her regularly. Vary your expressions deliberately — she is reading happy, surprised, and worried faces.
guide.watch: Clear preference for familiar faces; beginning to react differently to strangers.
guide.see: No differentiation between familiar and unfamiliar faces by 5 months.

guide.try: Keep face-to-face sessions to 5–10 focused minutes. Songs with actions and simple back-and-forth exchanges are ideal at this age.
guide.watch: She smiles, vocalises, and reaches toward you during play.
guide.see: Consistently unresponsive to social play — worth mentioning at your check-up.

guide.try: Build anticipation by pausing mid-game and making a ‘what’s next?’ face before continuing. This teaches her that interaction is reciprocal.
guide.watch: Fussing, reaching, or vocalising when an engaging activity or interaction stops.
guide.see: No emotional response to changes in social interaction by 6 months.

guide.try: Hold a baby-safe mirror at face level and narrate: ‘Who’s that? That’s you!’ Move it slightly and watch her track the reflection.
guide.watch: She reaches for the reflection, smiles at it, or vocalises in response.
guide.see: No visual interest in reflections or faces in a mirror by 5 months.

milestones.text: Explores objects with hands and mouth

guide.try: Offer varied textures — silicone, wood, cloth, rubber. Supervised mouthing is safe and important; remove only genuinely hazardous items.
guide.watch: She grasps objects, turns them over, and brings them to her mouth with clear intent.
guide.see: Not exploring objects with hands at all by 5 months.

guide.try: Drop a toy while she is watching and say ‘Where did it go?’ Pause a few seconds before retrieving — you are building early object permanence.
guide.watch: Her gaze shifts downward to where the object fell, even briefly.
guide.see: Not a major concern at this stage; object permanence solidifies more at 7–9 months.

relatedActivities.name: Peekaboo beginnings
relatedActivities.tagline: The first joke your baby ever understands.

activityIntro.headline: Rolling, reaching, and the start of a real back-and-forth.
activityIntro.desc: This is often the most joyful quarter of the first year. Sleep starts to consolidate, laughter is frequent, and your baby is becoming an active participant in play rather than a recipient of it. Rolling, grasping, and the beginnings of object permanence take centre stage.
activityIntro.note: Drooling and gum-chewing often signal early teething, sometimes months before a tooth appears. Cold, safe teethers help more than most remedies.

activities.name: Reach and grasp play
activities.tagline: Crossing the midline is a brain milestone disguised as a toy grab.
activities.why: Between 3–6 months, reaching becomes deliberate and accurate, and babies start reaching across their body's midline — a sign the two hemispheres of the brain are learning to coordinate. Grasping and mouthing objects is also how babies explore texture, weight, and shape long before they can describe any of it.
steps: Offer a light rattle or textured toy slightly to one side, encouraging the opposite hand to cross over and reach.
Let them mouth safe objects freely — this is exploration, not just teething.
Rotate a small set of textured toys every few days rather than offering everything at once.
activities.parentNote: "Everything goes in the mouth right now. That's not a bad habit to break — it's their primary way of learning what things are."
watchFor: Reaching across the body for an object — midline crossing developing
Transferring an object hand to hand — a key motor milestone around 5–6 months
Strong one-sided preference — mention at the next check-up if persistent

activities.name: Peekaboo beginnings
activities.tagline: The first joke your baby ever understands.
activities.why: Peekaboo is a baby's first encounter with the idea that something can disappear and reappear — an early rehearsal of object permanence, the understanding that things (and people) continue to exist even when out of sight. This concept fully matures later, but the groundwork starts here.
steps: Cover your face briefly with your hands or a light cloth, then reveal it with an animated "Peekaboo!"
Keep the hidden interval short at first — a second or two — and watch for anticipation building over repeats.
Let them "find" you rather than always revealing yourself — pause and see if they pull the cloth away.
activities.parentNote: "The delight on repeat ten is somehow bigger than repeat one. That's not you running out of new ideas — that's them mastering a prediction, and loving being right."
watchFor: Anticipatory smiling before your face reappears — prediction forming
Reaching to pull the cloth away themselves — active participation
Sustained belly laughs at the reveal — pure delight, enjoy it

activities.name: Rolling practice
activities.tagline: The first way they'll ever travel on their own.
activities.why: Rolling front-to-back and back-to-front, usually mastered between 4–6 months, is the first independent form of locomotion. It requires core strength, coordination between both sides of the body, and — often overlooked — real motivation, since most babies roll to reach something they want.
steps: Place an interesting toy just out of reach to one side during tummy time, encouraging a reach-and-roll.
From on their back, gently guide one knee across the body to help them feel the rolling motion, then let them try unassisted.
Give plenty of open, safe floor space and time — rolling can't be rushed, only invited.
activities.parentNote: "The first time they roll and surprise themselves, watch their face. That mix of shock and pride is a preview of every milestone still to come."
watchFor: Rolling one direction consistently but not the other — normal at first, keep practicing both
Getting "stuck" on their side and frustrated — offer a gentle nudge, don't over-rescue
No rolling in either direction by 6 months — worth flagging at the check-up

label: 7–9 months
title: On the move
note: Separation anxiety often starts here — completely normal! Peek-a-boo games help babies learn you always return.

guide.try: Practise sitting on the floor with cushions around her. Sit facing her and hold out toys — reaching for them builds the balance muscles needed.
guide.watch: Sits steadily for several seconds without hands on the floor for support.
guide.see: Cannot sit even briefly with hands for support by 9 months.

guide.try: Place enticing toys just beyond reach during tummy time. Some babies bottom-shuffle or roll instead of crawling — all are valid ways of getting mobile.
guide.watch: Any method of moving forward on her own — crawl, creep, shuffle, or roll.
guide.see: No independent mobility of any kind by 12 months.

guide.try: Scatter small safe objects (cereal puffs, soft diced fruit) on the high-chair tray and let her pick them up. This is perfect fine motor practice.
guide.watch: She picks up small objects using thumb and index finger rather than the whole palm.
guide.see: Only using a whole-palm rake grasp with no pincer development by 12 months.

guide.try: Place her hands on the edge of a low, stable table or sofa and encourage her to pull up. Stay close — falls are part of learning but shouldn’t be hard ones.
guide.watch: She uses furniture or your hands to pull herself up to standing.
guide.see: No attempt to bear weight on legs by 9 months.

relatedActivities.name: Supported sitting play
relatedActivities.tagline: Free hands change everything.

guide.try: Play imitation games: clap, bang, wave — then wait and watch. The game of ‘your turn, my turn’ directly builds her imitation ability.
guide.watch: She copies simple sounds and gestures you make, even with a short delay.
guide.see: No imitation of sounds or gestures by 9 months.

guide.try: Use ‘no’ sparingly and consistently, with a firm tone and clear body language. Reserve it for genuinely important moments so it carries meaning.
guide.watch: She pauses, looks at you, or changes her behaviour when you say ‘no’ firmly.
guide.see: Not a standalone concern; flag if combined with limited understanding of words or gestures.

guide.try: Wave consistently whenever someone leaves. Pair it with ‘bye-bye’ every time and make it a cheerful, dramatic gesture she will want to copy.
guide.watch: She begins waving her hand (even approximately) at departure or when she hears ‘bye-bye’.
guide.see: No waving or pointing gestures by 12 months.

guide.try: Point to yourself and say ‘mama’ or ‘dada’ often. Label everything around her — more words heard means faster language development.
guide.watch: She produces these sounds frequently in babble — they don’t need to be directed at you yet.
guide.see: No babbling with varied consonants (ma, da, ba, ga) by 9 months.

relatedActivities.name: Babbling back-and-forth
relatedActivities.tagline: "Bababa" is the rough draft of every word that follows.

guide.try: Never force contact with new people. Hold her in your arms while engaging warmly with the stranger yourself — she takes her cue from your calm.
guide.watch: Clinging, fussing, or crying with unfamiliar adults is completely normal and healthy at this age.
guide.see: No stranger anxiety at all — complete indifference to unfamiliar people — is worth noting.

guide.try: Ensure she has consistent primary caregivers. Brief separations with a reliable person practise the healthy attachment she is building.
guide.watch: Clear preference for familiar carers; settles more quickly with them.
guide.see: No preference for any caregiver at all by 9 months.

guide.try: Play peek-a-boo many times a day — behind your hands, a cloth, a doorframe. This game directly teaches that things exist even when hidden.
guide.watch: She anticipates the reveal with smiles or excited vocalisations before you reappear.
guide.see: No enjoyment of or engagement with simple interactive games by 9 months.

guide.try: Show her a toy then hide it under a cloth while she watches. Lift the cloth with a ‘found it!’ and repeat — you are directly building object permanence.
guide.watch: She pulls the cloth away or looks in the right place for the hidden toy.
guide.see: No searching for hidden objects by 9 months.

guide.try: Give her two objects that make satisfying sounds when banged — wooden spoons, cups, blocks. Demonstrate and let her copy.
guide.watch: She purposefully brings objects together with clear cause-and-effect exploration.
guide.see: Not typically isolated; flag if hand use is very limited overall.

guide.try: Point at objects yourself constantly, naming them: ‘Look — dog!’ She is learning to use pointing as communication.
guide.watch: She extends her index finger toward things she wants or finds interesting.
guide.see: No pointing or reaching toward objects of interest by 12 months.

relatedActivities.name: Food exploration
relatedActivities.tagline: Mealtime is a sensory lesson before it's ever a nutrition one.

activityIntro.headline: Sitting up, and reaching for the world.
activityIntro.desc: Independent sitting frees the hands for serious exploration, babbling starts sounding like real syllables, and solid foods enter the picture. Stranger awareness — a healthy sign of attachment — often appears around this time, alongside the beginnings of crawling.
activityIntro.note: A sudden clinginess to one caregiver or wariness of unfamiliar faces around 8–9 months is a normal developmental stage (stranger anxiety), not a regression.

activities.name: Supported sitting play
activities.tagline: Free hands change everything.
activities.why: Sitting independently, typically mastered between 6–8 months, is a major shift: it frees both hands for manipulating objects instead of using them for balance. This new vantage point also changes how babies see and engage with their environment, accelerating cognitive and fine motor development together.
steps: Sit baby with light support (cushions behind, or between your legs) and place toys in front for reaching practice.
Gradually reduce support as their balance improves, staying close to catch gentle tips.
Offer toys slightly to the side to encourage twisting and weight-shifting while seated.
activities.parentNote: "The topples are part of it. A soft landing and a shrug teaches them that falling isn't failure — it's just information for the next try."
watchFor: Using hands to catch a fall (a "protective reflex") — an important safety skill developing
Sitting briefly without any support — a big milestone worth celebrating
No independent sitting attempts by 9 months — worth mentioning to your pediatrician

activities.name: Babbling back-and-forth
activities.tagline: "Bababa" is the rough draft of every word that follows.
activities.why: Babbling — repeated consonant-vowel sounds like "bababa" or "dadada" — typically emerges around 6–7 months and is a strong predictor of later speech development. Responding to babbling as though it were a real conversation reinforces the same serve-and-return dynamic that built early bonding, now aimed squarely at language.
steps: When your baby babbles, respond as if they said something meaningful: "Oh really? Tell me more!"
Repeat their sounds back, then add a real word connected to context — if they say "mamama" while reaching for you, say "Yes, mama's here."
Leave pauses for them to babble again — the back-and-forth matters more than any single exchange.
activities.parentNote: "You are not imagining a conversation that isn't there. Babbling with real turn-taking is genuinely how they're rehearsing language before they have the words for it."
watchFor: Repeated consonant-vowel strings — babbling on schedule
Different babbling "tunes" for different needs — early tone/intent variation
No babbling at all by 9 months — worth discussing with your pediatrician

activities.name: Food exploration
activities.tagline: Mealtime is a sensory lesson before it's ever a nutrition one.
activities.why: Starting solids around 6 months (alongside continued milk feeds) introduces new tastes, textures, and the fine motor challenge of self-feeding. Letting babies explore food with their hands — even messily — builds the oral-motor skills needed for chewing and speech, and supports a healthy relationship with food from the start.
steps: Offer soft, appropriately-sized pieces of food (steamed vegetable sticks, ripe fruit) they can hold themselves — check current guidance on size and shape for safety.
Let them explore with their hands and face before worrying about how much goes in — this is exploration, not just eating.
Sit them at the family table when possible; babies learn eating behavior largely by watching others.
activities.parentNote: "The mess is the lesson, not a side effect of it. A baby covered in mashed sweet potato is doing exactly what they're supposed to be doing at this age."
watchFor: Bringing food to their own mouth — self-feeding developing
Gagging (normal, a protective reflex) versus choking (silent, urgent) — know the difference
Strong refusal of all textures past a few tries — mention to your pediatrician

label: 10–12 months
title: Almost toddler
note: First words vary hugely. 1–3 clear words by 12 months is typical — point and name things constantly to boost vocabulary.

guide.try: Practise letting go of support for a second or two while you stay close. Praise every attempt even if she immediately sits back down.
guide.watch: She releases her hold and balances unsupported for at least 1–2 seconds.
guide.see: Cannot bear weight on legs at all by 12 months.

guide.try: Arrange stable furniture so she has a safe route to cruise along. Place tempting toys at the far end to give her a goal.
guide.watch: She moves sideways along furniture with growing confidence and fewer stumbles.
guide.see: No attempt to move along furniture or bear weight by 12 months.

guide.try: Sit on the floor a step away, hold out your hands, and call her. Don’t rush — walking can arrive any time between 9 and 18 months and still be normal.
guide.watch: One, two, then a few steps independently before sitting or falling.
guide.see: Not walking independently by 18 months is the clinical threshold for evaluation.

guide.try: Offer appropriately sized soft pieces (banana, well-cooked vegetables) on the high-chair tray. Let mealtimes be messy — it is fine motor work.
guide.watch: She picks up pieces intentionally and brings them to her mouth with growing accuracy.
guide.see: Not a developmental red flag alone; flag if combined with very limited hand coordination.

relatedActivities.name: Cruise the furniture
relatedActivities.tagline: Every couch is a practice bar right now.

guide.try: Label objects and people consistently every day. When she says an approximation of a word, enthusiastically confirm it: ‘Yes! Ball!’
guide.watch: At least one word used consistently to mean the same thing — doesn’t need perfect pronunciation.
guide.see: No words with consistent meaning by 16 months warrants a speech check.

guide.try: Give simple commands with gesture: ‘Give me the cup’ while holding out your hand. Pair words with clear actions every time at first.
guide.watch: She follows the instruction without you needing to physically guide her.
guide.see: No response to simple commands (with or without gesture) by 12 months.

guide.try: Respond meaningfully to every gesture she makes — if she points, name the object and respond. This teaches her gestures have power.
guide.watch: Pointing, reaching, waving, and head-shaking used with clear communicative intent.
guide.see: No communicative gestures at all by 12 months.

relatedActivities.name: Pointing and naming
relatedActivities.tagline: A pointed finger is a question: "what's that?"

guide.try: Demonstrate simple actions: stir a bowl, talk on a toy phone, drive a toy car. Then hand it to her and wait — imitation is how she learns.
guide.watch: She copies actions she has seen adults do, even with a delay of hours.
guide.see: No imitative play at all by 12 months.

guide.try: Model affection openly — hug, kiss, cuddle her freely. She learns emotional expression by seeing it, not just receiving it.
guide.watch: Leans in for hugs, pats faces, shows physical affection spontaneously.
guide.see: Consistently avoids physical affection with familiar people by 12 months.

guide.try: Stay consistent and calm — if you react dramatically, she will repeat the behaviour for the reaction. A neutral response is often most effective.
guide.watch: She looks at your face before, during, or after doing something she knows is off-limits.
guide.see: This is completely healthy developmental behaviour — not a concern.

guide.try: Play hide-and-seek with toys under cups or cloths. Progress from transparent to opaque to make it gradually harder.
guide.watch: She looks in the right location for a hidden toy, even when moved to a new spot.
guide.see: No search for hidden objects by 12 months.

guide.try: Offer toys with clear cause-and-effect: drop things into containers, bang drums, press buttons that light up. Let her repeat to her heart’s content.
guide.watch: She deliberately repeats actions that produce interesting results.
guide.see: No intentional cause-and-effect play by 12 months.

guide.try: Give her a container and small blocks and demonstrate dropping them in. Say ‘in!’ each time, then let her try independently.
guide.watch: She drops objects into containers with clear intent, and may tip them out to repeat.
guide.see: No object manipulation or container play by 12 months.

relatedActivities.name: Copy-cat games
relatedActivities.tagline: Waving bye-bye is their first party trick, and their first real learning strategy.

activityIntro.headline: Standing at the edge of walking and talking.
activityIntro.desc: The final quarter of year one is full of "almosts" — cruising along furniture before walking, single meaningful words before sentences, pointing before naming. Imitation becomes a powerful new learning tool: your baby is watching everything you do and starting to try it themselves.
activityIntro.note: There's wide normal variation in when babies walk (anywhere from 9–18 months) and say a first word (8–14 months). Range, not a race.

activities.name: Cruise the furniture
activities.tagline: Every couch is a practice bar right now.
activities.why: "Cruising" — walking sideways while holding onto furniture — is the bridge between crawling and independent walking. It builds the balance, leg strength, and confidence needed to eventually let go. Most babies cruise for weeks or months before taking their first unsupported steps.
steps: Arrange sturdy, low furniture (that won't tip) in a loose path so baby can cruise from one piece to the next.
Sit a small, appealing distance away with arms open, encouraging a step or two toward you.
Let go of their hands during supported walking for a second at a time, building independent balance gradually.
activities.parentNote: "The wobble before the first free step looks like it might end in a fall every single time. Mostly it doesn't. Stay close, but let them find their own balance."
watchFor: Standing briefly without holding on — a strong signal walking is close
Bending knees to lower themselves down safely — good body awareness
No pulling to stand or weight-bearing on legs by 12 months — worth flagging to your pediatrician

activities.name: Pointing and naming
activities.tagline: A pointed finger is a question: "what's that?"
activities.why: Pointing to show or request something, usually emerging around 9–12 months, reflects "joint attention" — the ability to share focus on an object with another person. This is considered one of the strongest early predictors of later language development, since naming what's pointed at directly builds vocabulary.
steps: When your baby points or looks intently at something, follow their gaze and name it immediately: "Yes! That's the dog."
Point at things yourself during walks or play, narrating simply.
Repeat favorite words often and in different contexts — hearing "dog" many times, in many places, builds the concept, not just the sound.
activities.parentNote: "Follow their point before you redirect it. What they're curious about right now is the most useful vocabulary lesson you'll ever get — better than any flashcard."
watchFor: Pointing to request an object out of reach — intentional communication
Looking at you after pointing, checking you noticed — joint attention in action
No pointing or gesture use by 12–14 months — worth discussing with your pediatrician

activities.name: Copy-cat games
activities.tagline: Waving bye-bye is their first party trick, and their first real learning strategy.
activities.why: Imitation — copying gestures like waving, clapping, or blowing a kiss — becomes a major learning tool in this quarter. It relies on mirror neuron activity and marks a shift toward learning by observation, the same mechanism that will later drive learning language, manners, and countless everyday skills.
steps: Model simple gestures clearly and often — wave when someone leaves, clap when something goes well.
Gently guide their hands through the motion once or twice if they don't copy right away, then let them try alone.
Celebrate any attempt, even a rough approximation — enthusiasm reinforces the behavior.
activities.parentNote: "The first unprompted wave to a stranger in the lift will make your whole day. Let it. These small performances are huge developmental leaps in disguise."
watchFor: Waving or clapping without a prompt — imitation becoming spontaneous
Copying facial expressions or sounds — expanding imitation repertoire
No imitation of any gestures by 12 months — worth mentioning at the check-up

label: 12–15 months
title: Early stepper
note: Walking and talking often trade off — a child pouring energy into one skill may pause on the other for a few weeks. Completely typical.

guide.try: Practise letting go of support for a second or two while staying close. Praise every attempt even if she sits straight back down.
guide.watch: She releases her hold and balances unsupported for at least 1–2 seconds.
guide.see: Cannot bear weight on legs at all by 15 months.

guide.try: Sit a step away, hold out your hands, and call her. Walking can arrive anywhere from 9 to 18 months and still be normal — there's no need to rush it.
guide.watch: One, two, then a few steps independently before sitting or falling.
guide.see: No attempt at independent steps by 15 months, alongside no cruising either.

guide.try: Demonstrate stacking one block on another slowly, then hand her the next block and wait. A flat, stable surface makes this much easier.
guide.watch: She places one block on top of another, even if it topples right after.
guide.see: No attempt to stack or manipulate blocks by 15 months.

relatedActivities.name: Walking adventures
relatedActivities.tagline: Every wobbly step is a small triumph over gravity.

relatedActivities.name: Scribble time
relatedActivities.tagline: The first mark on paper is the first draft of handwriting.

guide.try: Label objects and people consistently every day. When she says an approximation of a word, confirm it warmly: ‘Yes! Ball!’
guide.watch: At least one word used consistently to mean the same thing — pronunciation doesn't need to be perfect.
guide.see: No words with consistent meaning by 15 months.

guide.try: Respond meaningfully every time she points — name the object and hand it over. This teaches her that pointing has real communicative power.
guide.watch: She extends her index finger toward something she wants, checking your face for a response.
guide.see: No pointing or reaching to communicate by 15 months.

milestones.text: Follows a one-step command with a gesture

guide.try: Pair a simple instruction with a clear gesture: ‘Give me the cup’ while holding out your hand.
guide.watch: She completes the action when word and gesture are given together.
guide.see: No response to simple requests, even gestured, by 15 months.

relatedActivities.name: Simple instruction games
relatedActivities.tagline: "Give me the ball" is a bigger ask than it sounds.

milestones.text: Shows clear preference for a primary caregiver

guide.try: This is healthy attachment, not favouritism to worry about. Continue building warm one-to-one time with every caregiver in the family.
guide.watch: She reaches for, calms fastest with, or seeks out one particular person when upset.
guide.see: No differentiated attachment to any caregiver by 15 months.

guide.try: Play simple copying games — clap, wave, pat your head — and pause for her to try. Repetition across many days is what makes it click.
guide.watch: She copies a simple gesture you make, even a few seconds later.
guide.see: No imitation of any gestures or actions by 15 months.

milestones.text: Seeks comfort when hurt or scared

guide.try: Respond promptly and warmly every time she's distressed. Reliable comfort now builds the security she'll draw on for independence later.
guide.watch: She reaches for or crawls/walks toward a caregiver when hurt, tired, or startled.
guide.see: No seeking of comfort from any caregiver when distressed by 15 months.

milestones.text: Finds an object after watching it hidden

guide.try: Hide a toy under a cup while she watches, then say ‘where did it go?’ and let her search.
guide.watch: She lifts the cup or looks in the right place for the hidden toy.
guide.see: No searching for hidden objects by 15 months.

milestones.text: Explores objects by turning, banging, dropping

guide.try: Offer a variety of safe household objects — measuring cups, wooden spoons — alongside toys. Let her investigate at her own pace.
guide.watch: She turns objects over, bangs them together, or drops them repeatedly with clear curiosity.
guide.see: No exploratory object play at all by 15 months.

milestones.text: Drinks from an open cup with help

guide.try: Offer a small amount of water in a small open cup, hands over hands at first. Expect spills — this is normal practice, not a mess to prevent.
guide.watch: She tips the cup toward her mouth and swallows with your hands guiding.
guide.see: No interest in or attempt at cup drinking by 15 months, alongside other feeding concerns.

activityIntro.headline: First steps, first words.
activityIntro.desc: Somewhere in this quarter, most toddlers take their first unsupported steps and say their first clear word beyond "mama" or "dada." Following simple one-step instructions and early mark-making with a crayon round out a quarter defined by new independence.
activityIntro.note: Walking and talking often trade off — a toddler pouring all their energy into one skill may pause on the other for a few weeks. That's typical, not a warning sign.

activities.name: Walking adventures
activities.tagline: Every wobbly step is a small triumph over gravity.
activities.why: Independent walking, typically arriving between 9–18 months, dramatically changes a toddler's relationship with their environment — suddenly they can approach what interests them rather than waiting to be carried there. Varied surfaces (grass, carpet, gentle slopes) build balance and confidence faster than flat floors alone.
steps: Offer safe, varied terrain to practice on — a grassy patch, a soft rug, a gentle ramp.
Set up simple obstacle courses with cushions to step over or around.
Resist holding both hands constantly — one hand, or none, builds independent balance faster.
activities.parentNote: "The falls will outnumber the steps for a while. Let the falls happen on soft ground and skip the rescue reflex when you can — recovering from a stumble is its own valuable lesson."
watchFor: Taking several steps between supports — independent walking emerging
Squatting to pick something up without falling over — improving balance
No walking attempts at all by 18 months — worth discussing with your pediatrician

activities.name: Simple instruction games
activities.tagline: "Give me the ball" is a bigger ask than it sounds.
activities.why: Following simple one-step directions ("give me the cup," "come here") shows that receptive language — understanding — is running well ahead of expressive language (speaking), which is normal and expected at this age. Practicing this builds listening comprehension and the confidence of successfully completing a task.
steps: Give one clear, simple instruction at a time: "Bring me the ball," paired with a gesture if needed.
Celebrate success warmly and specifically: "You brought the ball! You listened!"
Turn it into a game — hiding an object and asking them to find and bring it.
activities.parentNote: "They understand far more than they can say right now. Talk to them like they follow every word, because mostly, they do."
watchFor: Following a one-step instruction without a gesture cue — comprehension deepening
A small but growing spoken vocabulary — expressive language catching up
No response to simple requests or their own name by 15 months — worth flagging

activities.name: Scribble time
activities.tagline: The first mark on paper is the first draft of handwriting.
activities.why: Grasping a large crayon and making random marks on paper builds the hand strength and grip patterns that eventually lead to controlled drawing and writing years from now. At this age it's entirely about the sensory feedback and cause-and-effect — mark appears, hand moved — not about the picture.
steps: Offer large, chunky, non-toxic crayons and big sheets of paper taped down so they don't slide.
Let them scribble freely without correcting their grip yet — that refinement comes later.
Narrate what you see: "Big lines! You're going fast there."
activities.parentNote: "It will look like nothing. It is, in fact, the very beginning of every letter they will ever write."
watchFor: A fist grip on the crayon — completely typical at this stage
Marks appearing with intentional arm movement, not just random contact — motor control building
No interest in holding or using any tool by 18 months — worth mentioning at a check-up

label: 15–18 months
title: Confident walker
note: Vocabulary and physical skill rarely progress at the same pace — a child racing ahead in one area is often taking their time in the other.

guide.try: Give her plenty of open floor space and let her practise without constantly holding her hands. Uneven surfaces like grass build balance fast.
guide.watch: Confident walking with fewer falls and better balance week over week.
guide.see: Not walking independently at all by 18 months.

guide.try: Allow supervised climbing on low, stable surfaces — a small foam step or low platform works well.
guide.watch: She manoeuvres herself onto a low sofa or step with some effort.
guide.see: Extremely limited gross motor activity overall by 18 months.

guide.try: Tape paper to the floor and offer a large, chunky crayon. The floor position gives more control than a table at this age.
guide.watch: Any intentional mark-making, even random lines — the intent is what matters, not the picture.
guide.see: No interest in mark-making tools or very weak hand grip by 18 months.

relatedActivities.name: Block stacking and knocking down
relatedActivities.tagline: Building it up is the skill. Knocking it down is the joy.

guide.try: Read board books daily and name every picture. Expand what she says — if she says ‘dog’, you say ‘big brown dog!’
guide.watch: New words appearing steadily, even rough approximations that are used consistently.
guide.see: Fewer than 3 words by 18 months, or any loss of previously used words, warrants evaluation.

milestones.text: Points to at least one body part on request

guide.try: Play naming games during bath time or dressing: ‘where's your nose?’ Point to your own first, then hers.
guide.watch: She points to or touches a named body part, even if only one or two consistently.
guide.see: No response to any ‘where's your…’ questions by 18 months.

milestones.text: Combines a gesture with a word to request

guide.try: Model the pairing yourself — say ‘up’ while reaching your arms up. She'll pick up the combination through repetition.
guide.watch: She reaches or points while saying a word, rather than using either alone.
guide.see: No combined gesture-and-word requests by 18 months.

relatedActivities.name: Naming walk
relatedActivities.tagline: The world is a vocabulary list waiting to be labeled.

guide.try: Offer controlled choices — ‘the red cup or the blue cup?’ — which satisfies the autonomy drive while you stay in charge.
guide.watch: Strong assertions of ownership and preference — this is healthy, not defiance.
guide.see: This is healthy developmental behaviour — not a concern on its own.

milestones.text: Plays alone contentedly for short stretches

guide.try: Set up an inviting, safe play space and resist jumping in immediately. A few minutes of independent play is a genuine skill being built.
guide.watch: She engages with a toy or activity on her own for several minutes without needing you.
guide.see: Cannot settle into any independent play at all by 18 months.

guide.try: Model affection openly — hugs, kisses, gentle pats — so she has a rich example to draw from.
guide.watch: She leans in for a hug, pats your face, or offers a spontaneous kiss.
guide.see: Consistently avoids or resists all physical affection by 18 months.

relatedActivities.name: Yes/no and simple choices
relatedActivities.tagline: A little control now prevents a lot of tantrums later.

milestones.text: Uses a spoon with some spilling

guide.try: Offer a small spoon and thick foods like yoghurt that stick well. Expect mess — it's the practice that matters, not the outcome.
guide.watch: She scoops and brings the spoon to her mouth, even if half the food falls off.
guide.see: No attempt to self-feed with any utensil by 18 months.

milestones.text: Matches simple shapes to a shape sorter

guide.try: Start with just one or two shapes at a time rather than the full set. Guide her hand through the motion once before stepping back.
guide.watch: She attempts to fit a shape into its matching hole, even with trial and error.
guide.see: No interest in or attempt at shape-fitting toys by 18 months.

milestones.text: Points to named pictures in a book

guide.try: Ask ‘where's the cat?’ while reading and give her a moment to point before turning the page.
guide.watch: She points to a named picture in a familiar book with increasing accuracy.
guide.see: No response to simple picture-naming questions by 18 months.

activityIntro.headline: Words multiplying, will growing.
activityIntro.desc: Vocabulary starts climbing steadily, and so does opinion. Parallel play alongside other children begins to appear, running and climbing get more confident, and the first real tantrums show up as your toddler's feelings outpace their words.
activityIntro.note: Tantrums at this age are neurological, not manipulative — the emotional brain is fully online while the regulating "brakes" of the prefrontal cortex are years from mature. Calm co-regulation, not punishment, is what actually helps.

activities.name: Naming walk
activities.tagline: The world is a vocabulary list waiting to be labeled.
activities.why: Vocabulary often grows from a handful of words at 15 months to dozens by 18 months. Hearing object names repeatedly, in context, and paired with pointing or touching, is one of the most reliable ways to build that vocabulary — far more effective than passive screen exposure at this age.
steps: On a walk or around the house, name objects as you both notice them: "Tree. Big tree. Car. Red car."
Pause after naming something to let them attempt to repeat it, even imperfectly.
Revisit the same objects across different days — repetition across contexts locks words in.
activities.parentNote: "You will say the word 'dog' more times this month than you've said it in years. Every single repetition is doing quiet, real work."
watchFor: Attempting to repeat new words, even approximately — active vocabulary building
Using a word correctly days after first hearing it — retention happening
Fewer than 10 words by 18 months — worth discussing with your pediatrician

activities.name: Yes/no and simple choices
activities.tagline: A little control now prevents a lot of tantrums later.
activities.why: Toddlers this age are developing a strong drive for autonomy well before they have the language or self-control to express it smoothly — a mismatch that fuels many tantrums. Offering small, genuine choices ("red cup or blue cup?") satisfies the need for control within limits you're comfortable with.
steps: Offer two acceptable options rather than an open-ended question: "Blue socks or yellow socks?"
Let them point or gesture if words aren't there yet — the choice-making matters more than the vocabulary.
Honor the choice once made, even if it's not what you'd have picked — this is where the trust in the system gets built.
activities.parentNote: "It feels slower to offer choices instead of just deciding for them. It is slower. It's also the thing that turns a rushed morning into a cooperative one, more often than not."
watchFor: Pointing or gesturing to indicate a preference — decision-making in action
Fewer meltdowns during routines with built-in choices — autonomy need being met
Frustration when no choice is offered at all — a cue to build in more, where safe

activities.name: Block stacking and knocking down
activities.tagline: Building it up is the skill. Knocking it down is the joy.
activities.why: Stacking two or three blocks, usually achieved by 18 months, requires fine motor precision, hand-eye coordination, and patience with failure — the tower falls a lot before it stands. The satisfaction of knocking it down afterward is a healthy, safe way to explore cause and effect and release energy.
steps: Start by stacking one or two blocks yourself, then hand the next one over for them to place.
Let the tower topple naturally, or hand over the "knocking down" job explicitly — both are valid play.
Gradually invite them to build the whole tower themselves, offering blocks one at a time.
activities.parentNote: "Watching them knock down their own careful work with total glee, then immediately want to build it again, is a small but perfect lesson in resilience."
watchFor: Stacking two or more blocks before it topples — fine motor milestone
Rebuilding after a fall without frustration spiraling — early persistence
No interest in stacking or handling small objects at all — mention if it persists

label: 18–21 months
title: Little explorer
note: Parallel play — playing near, not with, other children — is the developmentally correct stage right now, not a social delay.

guide.try: Encourage active outdoor play on grass or soft ground, which is more forgiving than hard indoor floors for a still-wobbly run.
guide.watch: She moves faster than a walk with a brief flight phase — at least one foot off the ground.
guide.see: Not walking steadily by 21 months, or falling in a way that isn't improving.

guide.try: Place a large, soft ball right by her feet and demonstrate a gentle kick first. Large slow balls are far easier to connect with than small ones.
guide.watch: She swings a leg and makes intentional contact with the ball.
guide.see: No ball play or lower-body coordination by 21 months.

milestones.text: Walks up steps holding a rail, both feet per step

guide.try: Hold her hand or let her use a rail on real stairs when supervised. Narrate: ‘up, up, up.’
guide.watch: She climbs steps one at a time, both feet landing on each step before the next.
guide.see: No attempt to climb stairs with support by 21 months.

guide.try: Narrate your day constantly — more words heard consistently means faster vocabulary growth. Name everything you touch, use, or pass.
guide.watch: New words appearing weekly, even rough or partial pronunciations used consistently.
guide.see: Fewer than 10 words by 21 months, or any loss of words, warrants evaluation.

milestones.text: Combines a word and gesture into simple requests

guide.try: Respond to combined requests warmly and promptly — ‘up’ plus reaching arms means she wants to be picked up. Confirm it in words back.
guide.watch: She reliably pairs a gesture with a word to make her wants clear.
guide.see: No combined communicative attempts of any kind by 21 months.

milestones.text: Follows a familiar 2-step routine with prompting

guide.try: Use the same two-step routine daily — ‘get your shoes, bring them here’ — so the pattern becomes predictable.
guide.watch: She completes both familiar steps with a gentle reminder partway through.
guide.see: Not following even a one-step instruction by 21 months.

relatedActivities.name: Name that picture
relatedActivities.tagline: "What's this?" is now their favorite game, on repeat.

guide.try: Arrange playdates with similar toys for each child, side by side. Don't force sharing or turn-taking yet — this stage is about comfortable proximity.
guide.watch: She plays contentedly near another child, aware of them without directly interacting.
guide.see: Complete disinterest in or distress around other children by 21 months.

milestones.text: Shows a wide range of clear emotions

guide.try: Name emotions as they show up: ‘you're so happy about that!’ or ‘that made you upset.’ This is early groundwork for later self-regulation.
guide.watch: Clear, distinguishable expressions of joy, frustration, fear, and affection.
guide.see: Very flat or limited emotional expression by 21 months.

guide.try: Stay calm and consistent — dramatic reactions tend to reinforce the very behaviour you're hoping to reduce.
guide.watch: She looks at your face while doing something she knows is off-limits, gauging your reaction.
guide.see: This is healthy, expected developmental behaviour — not a concern.

milestones.text: Sorts objects into two groups with help

guide.try: Sort laundry together — ‘socks here, shirts there’ — guiding her hand at first before letting her try alone.
guide.watch: She places an object in the correct group with a prompt or light guidance.
guide.see: No sorting attempt at all, even with heavy guidance, by 21 months.

guide.try: Start with single-piece inset puzzles before moving to 2–3 pieces. Guide her hand through the motion once before stepping back.
guide.watch: She attempts and sometimes completes a simple puzzle with trial and error.
guide.see: No problem-solving play with fit-together toys by 21 months.

guide.try: Model simple pretend actions — feeding a doll, pushing a toy car — and hand her the toy to try herself.
guide.watch: She applies a pretend action to a toy that she's seen applied to herself, like feeding a doll.
guide.see: No pretend or symbolic play at all by 21 months.

activityIntro.headline: A vocabulary explosion, and the first pretend play.
activityIntro.desc: Many toddlers go from dozens of words to what feels like hundreds within weeks — the so-called "word spurt."
