Two-word phrases begin appearing, pretend play (feeding a doll, "talking" on a toy phone) starts in earnest, and "me do it" becomes a frequent declaration of independence.
activityIntro.note: The push for independence and the frustration when it isn't achieved yet can look like defiance. It's usually just a toddler whose ambition has outpaced their ability — patience over correction goes a long way here.

activities.name: Pretend play starter kit
activities.tagline: Feeding a stuffed bear is a bigger deal than it looks.
activities.why: Early pretend play — feeding a doll, "talking" on a toy phone, pretending a block is a car — is a major cognitive leap: it shows a toddler can hold two realities in mind at once, the real and the imagined. This symbolic thinking is closely linked to later language and problem-solving skills.
steps: Offer simple open-ended props — a soft doll, a play spoon, a toy phone — without a rigid "right way" to use them.
Model a simple pretend action yourself first: "Let's feed teddy his lunch," then hand over the spoon.
Follow their lead once they start — join whatever scenario they invent, however small.
activities.parentNote: "The first time they hold a banana to their ear like a phone, something clicks — you're watching imagination turn on in real time."
watchFor: Using one object to represent another — symbolic thinking emerging
Repeating the same pretend scenario often — a favorite script forming
No pretend play at all by 24 months — worth mentioning at a check-up

activities.name: Name that picture
activities.tagline: "What's this?" is now their favorite game, on repeat.
activities.why: During the vocabulary explosion, picture books become an efficient way to introduce dozens of new words quickly, in a low-pressure, repeatable format. Toddlers often want the same book read the exact same way, over and over — a preference for predictability that actually reinforces learning.
steps: Choose simple picture books with one clear object per page.
Point and name, then pause and ask "What's this?" — let them answer even with a sound or a point.
Re-read favorites as often as requested, resisting the urge to introduce something "new" too soon.
activities.parentNote: "By the fortieth read of the same book, you'll have it memorized. That repetition is exactly what's building their vocabulary — the boredom is yours, not theirs."
watchFor: Pointing to a named picture correctly — receptive vocabulary growing
Attempting the word themselves before you say it — anticipatory recall
Two-word combinations appearing ("more book," "big dog") — early sentence structure

activities.name: Let them do it
activities.tagline: "Me do it" is a request worth honoring, even when it's slower.
activities.why: Attempting to self-feed with a spoon, push an arm through a sleeve, or take off their own socks builds fine motor skill and, just as importantly, a toddler's sense of competence. Doing things for them when they're eager to try erodes confidence over time, even though it's faster in the moment.
steps: Build in extra time for routines where independence is welcome — dressing, eating, washing hands.
Set them up to succeed: loose clothing, a spoon with a easy-grip handle, a low step to reach the sink.
Offer help only when asked, or after real struggle — narrate encouragement instead of taking over: "You're working hard on that sleeve."
activities.parentNote: "It will take three times as long and the shirt will end up backwards. Let it. The backwards shirt is the whole point — they did it themselves."
watchFor: Persisting through a task despite frustration — early resilience building
Asking for help only after genuinely trying — good self-awareness
Meltdowns when help is offered too soon — a sign to step back further next time

label: 21–24 months
title: Emerging talker
note: Two-word combinations often mark the end of this stretch — but the range of normal is genuinely wide, so don't compare timelines between children.

milestones.text: Runs with better control, stops without falling

guide.try: Give her open space to practise starting, running, and stopping — games like ‘red light, green light’ (even a very simple version) help.
guide.watch: She runs with more balance and can stop or change direction without toppling over.
guide.see: Not walking steadily or attempting to run at all by 24 months.

milestones.text: Jumps in place with both feet

guide.try: Demonstrate a small jump and count ‘1, 2, 3, jump!’ Make it playful — jumping on a soft mat or low step is a fun way to practise.
guide.watch: Both feet leave the ground together, even if it's a very small hop.
guide.see: No jumping attempts of any kind by 24 months.

milestones.text: Throws a ball with some direction

guide.try: Stand close and make it a turn-taking game — she throws, you roll it back. Soft, light balls work best.
guide.watch: She lifts her arm and releases the ball with some forward intent.
guide.see: Very limited arm coordination or no throwing attempts by 24 months.

relatedActivities.name: Ball play
relatedActivities.tagline: Kicking a ball is a full-body coordination lesson.

guide.try: Read daily and expand what she says — if she says ‘dog’, respond ‘big brown dog!’ This expansion technique measurably speeds vocabulary growth.
guide.watch: New words appearing regularly — even approximations count if used consistently.
guide.see: Fewer than 15 words by 24 months, or any vocabulary loss, warrants urgent evaluation.

guide.try: Model two-word combinations constantly — ‘more biscuit’, ‘daddy go’ — without requiring her to repeat them back.
guide.watch: Two words combined spontaneously and meaningfully, without prompting.
guide.see: No two-word combinations at all by 24 months.

milestones.text: Follows a two-step instruction without a gesture cue

guide.try: Give two-step instructions using words alone, without pointing or demonstrating — ‘get your shoes and bring them here.’
guide.watch: She completes both steps from words alone, without a gesture to guide her.
guide.see: Not following even a one-step verbal instruction by 24 months.

milestones.text: Shows empathy toward another child's distress

guide.try: Model empathy openly — comfort a crying stuffed animal, narrate feelings: ‘teddy's sad, let's give him a hug.’
guide.watch: She pats, hugs, or brings an object to a child or toy that seems upset.
guide.see: Not a standalone concern; worth noting only alongside other social delays.

milestones.text: Engages in brief cooperative play with an adult

guide.try: Play simple back-and-forth games — rolling a ball, building then knocking down a tower together.
guide.watch: She takes a turn, waits for yours, then takes another — even briefly.
guide.see: No interest in any turn-based interaction by 24 months.

guide.try: Stay calm and consistent through outbursts — name the feeling without trying to talk her out of it: ‘you're really frustrated.’
guide.watch: Intense reactions that still resolve within a reasonable time with comfort.
guide.see: This is typical at this age — not a concern on its own.

relatedActivities.name: Naming feelings
relatedActivities.tagline: "You're mad" is the first step toward "I'm mad."

milestones.text: Names a few familiar objects in books unprompted

guide.try: Point and name everything in picture books daily. Ask ‘what's that?’ and wait a moment before supplying the answer.
guide.watch: She names a picture in a book without you asking first.
guide.see: No naming of any pictures, even with prompting, by 24 months.

milestones.text: Sorts objects by one clear attribute

guide.try: Use everyday objects — sort socks by colour, blocks by size. Keep it to one category at a time and narrate what you're doing.
guide.watch: She groups two or more objects by a shared attribute like colour.
guide.see: No sorting or grouping behaviour at all by 24 months.

guide.try: Set up simple scenarios — pour pretend tea, feed a doll — and let her extend it in her own way.
guide.watch: She strings together two related pretend actions, like stirring then feeding.
guide.see: No pretend or symbolic play at all by 24 months warrants evaluation.

relatedActivities.name: Sort and match
relatedActivities.tagline: Grouping by color is early math, disguised as tidying up.

activityIntro.headline: Two-word sentences, and big feelings to match.
activityIntro.desc: Two-word combinations ("more milk," "mommy go") become common, sorting and categorizing objects by color or size emerges, and emotions get bigger and faster than the words to express them. Some toddlers show early signs of potty readiness this quarter, though timelines vary widely.
activityIntro.note: The "terrible twos" label undersells what's really happening: a toddler with strong feelings, growing independence, and still-limited language and impulse control. Naming feelings, not just managing behavior, is the more useful long game.

activities.name: Sort and match
activities.tagline: Grouping by color is early math, disguised as tidying up.
activities.why: Sorting objects by a single attribute — color, size, or shape — is an early form of categorical thinking, a cognitive skill that underlies later math and logical reasoning. It also builds the vocabulary of comparison ("big," "small," "same," "different") that toddlers are just starting to use.
steps: Gather a small set of objects that differ in one clear way — red blocks vs. blue blocks, big spoons vs. small spoons.
Sort a couple yourself first, narrating: "Red one goes here. Blue one goes there."
Hand over the next object and let them choose where it goes, correcting gently only if asked.
activities.parentNote: "Sorting the laundry, the toy bin, the snack bowl — you don't need a special activity kit. Real household sorting works just as well as any toy."
watchFor: Sorting by one clear attribute consistently — categorical thinking developing
Using comparison words like "big" and "small" spontaneously — vocabulary applying to concepts
No interest in any sorting or matching play by 30 months — worth mentioning at a check-up

activities.name: Naming feelings
activities.tagline: "You're mad" is the first step toward "I'm mad."
activities.why: Toddlers experience intense emotions well before they have the vocabulary or self-control to manage them. Calmly naming what you observe — "You're frustrated because the tower fell" — builds emotional vocabulary and, over time, the self-awareness that underlies emotional regulation, one of the most important long-term skills you can support.
steps: During a meltdown, stay calm and name the feeling simply: "You're really upset right now."
Avoid trying to reason or negotiate mid-tantrum — the goal is co-regulation, not correction, in the moment.
Once calm, briefly revisit what happened in simple terms, reinforcing the vocabulary for next time.
activities.parentNote: "You are not rewarding the tantrum by staying calm and naming the feeling. You're teaching the only tool that will eventually replace it: words."
watchFor: Using a feeling word themselves during or after an upset — huge progress
Shorter recovery time from meltdowns over weeks — regulation improving
Frequent, prolonged tantrums with no discernible trigger — worth discussing with your pediatrician

activities.name: Ball play
activities.tagline: Kicking a ball is a full-body coordination lesson.
activities.why: Kicking and throwing a ball, both emerging in this quarter, require balance on one leg, whole-body coordination, and timing — considerably more complex than they look. Physical play like this is also an excellent, healthy outlet for the surplus energy and big emotions common at this age.
steps: Start with a large, soft ball and short distances, rolling it back and forth before progressing to kicking.
Show an exaggerated kicking or throwing motion yourself, then let them attempt it.
Celebrate contact and effort over accuracy — the coordination refines with practice, not correction.
activities.parentNote: "Ten minutes of kicking a ball around the garden does more for an overloaded toddler's mood than almost anything you could say to them."
watchFor: Kicking a stationary ball with some aim — gross motor coordination developing
Throwing overhand rather than just dropping the ball — a new motor pattern
Persistent difficulty with balance while kicking by 30 months — worth mentioning at a check-up

label: 24–27 months
title: Steady on their feet
note: Around two years, this quarter often brings a real leap in spoken vocabulary — sometimes seemingly overnight after a slower stretch.

milestones.text: Jumps forward with both feet together

guide.try: Demonstrate jumping off a low step with two feet together, counting ‘1, 2, 3, jump!’ A soft mat makes this safer to practise.
guide.watch: Both feet leave the ground together and land close to simultaneously.
guide.see: No jumping attempts at all by 27 months.

milestones.text: Kicks a ball with reasonable aim

guide.try: Set up a simple target — a box or a line — and encourage her to kick toward it. Large, slow balls are easiest to aim.
guide.watch: She kicks with some intentional direction, not just random contact.
guide.see: No ball play or lower-body coordination by 27 months.

milestones.text: Walks up stairs with one hand held, feet starting to alternate

guide.try: Hold one hand rather than both on real stairs, narrating: ‘this foot, now that foot.’
guide.watch: Occasional alternating steps appearing, even if she still favours one-foot-per-step some of the time.
guide.see: No stair navigation at all, even with full support, by 27 months.

relatedActivities.name: Jump and balance games
relatedActivities.tagline: Getting both feet off the ground at once is a genuine feat.

guide.try: Model two-word combinations in every routine — ‘more milk’, ‘big dog’, ‘daddy go’ — and respond naturally rather than asking her to repeat.
guide.watch: Two-word phrases used spontaneously and meaningfully in daily conversation.
guide.see: No two-word combinations at all by 27 months warrants a speech check.

milestones.text: Points correctly to several body parts

guide.try: Play naming games at bath time or dressing — ‘where's your nose? Your tummy?’ — pointing to your own first.
guide.watch: She reliably points to three or more named body parts.
guide.see: Cannot identify any body parts by name by 27 months.

guide.try: Answer every ‘what's that?’ simply and warmly, even when it's repetitive — the asking itself is real cognitive work.
guide.watch: Simple questions appearing spontaneously, even in a two-word form like ‘what dat?’
guide.see: No questioning behaviour of any kind by 27 months.

relatedActivities.name: Story time with choices
relatedActivities.tagline: Let them choose the page, and watch engagement double.

milestones.text: Begins brief cooperative play with a peer

guide.try: Set up simple joint activities — building a tower together — and narrate turn-taking: ‘my turn, your turn.’
guide.watch: She plays toward a shared goal with another child for a few minutes, even with adult support.
guide.see: No interest in cooperative activity with peers by 27 months.

milestones.text: Expresses a wider vocabulary of feelings

guide.try: Name feelings as they arise, using varied words — not just ‘happy’ and ‘sad’ but ‘excited’, ‘frustrated’, ‘tired’.
guide.watch: She uses or responds to a growing range of feeling words.
guide.see: Very limited emotional vocabulary or expression by 27 months.

guide.try: Celebrate her efforts specifically — ‘you built that all by yourself!’ — rather than generic praise.
guide.watch: She says or gestures ‘I did it!’ or seeks out your reaction after completing something.
guide.see: This is a healthy sign of self-awareness — not a concern.

guide.try: Count through daily routines — stairs, snacks, toys — out loud, consistently, so the sequence becomes familiar.
guide.watch: She recites ‘1, 2, 3’ in order, even without matching it to real objects yet.
guide.see: No number words or counting attempts at all by 27 months.

guide.try: Simple matching games — pairing identical picture cards or toys — build this skill quickly with repetition.
guide.watch: She pairs two identical items together with reasonable consistency.
guide.see: No matching behaviour at all, even with help, by 27 months.

milestones.text: Engages in pretend play with a story element

guide.try: Extend her pretend play with a simple narrative — ‘the doll is hungry, let's feed her, now she's sleepy.’
guide.watch: She links two or more pretend actions into a loose sequence.
guide.see: No pretend or symbolic play at all by 27 months warrants evaluation.

activityIntro.headline: Sentences, stories, and stronger legs.
activityIntro.desc: Three-word sentences start appearing, imaginative play gets more elaborate, and jumping with both feet off the ground is a new physical thrill. Sharing is still genuinely hard — not a character flaw, just a skill that takes years to build.
activityIntro.note: "Mine!" is a normal, expected phase reflecting a still-developing sense of ownership and self, not a sign of a selfish child. Modeling sharing works better than forcing it.

activities.name: Story time with choices
activities.tagline: Let them choose the page, and watch engagement double.
activities.why: By this age, toddlers can follow simple story sequences and often want to actively participate — choosing which book, predicting what happens next, or "reading" familiar pages aloud themselves. This active involvement builds narrative understanding, a skill closely tied to later reading comprehension.
steps: Offer two books and let them choose which one to read.
Pause at familiar or predictable moments and let them fill in the word or sound.
Ask simple questions about the pictures: "What do you think happens next?"
activities.parentNote: "When they correct you for skipping a page, that's not pedantry — that's a memory for story structure clicking into place."
watchFor: Filling in the missing word in a familiar story — memory and prediction developing
Using 3+ word sentences to describe a picture — sentence structure growing
No interest at all in books or stories by this age — worth mentioning at a check-up

activities.name: Imaginative play worlds
activities.tagline: A cardboard box is a spaceship, a cave, and a kitchen — sometimes all in one afternoon.
activities.why: Imaginative play grows more elaborate now — multi-step scenarios, simple role play, assigning roles to toys or people. This kind of play strengthens executive function (planning, flexible thinking) and social understanding, since acting out a scenario often means imagining what someone else might think or feel.
steps: Offer flexible props — dress-up clothes, a play kitchen, simple figures — rather than single-purpose toys.
Follow their lead rather than directing the scenario; ask questions to extend it: "What happens next in your story?"
Join briefly as a character if invited, then step back and let them run the scene.
activities.parentNote: "Resist tidying the elaborate 'restaurant' set up across the living room floor for a day or two if you can. The story often isn't finished."
watchFor: Multi-step pretend sequences — planning and sequencing developing
Assigning roles or feelings to toys — theory of mind emerging
Play that's exclusively repetitive with no variation over months — worth mentioning at a check-up

activities.name: Jump and balance games
activities.tagline: Getting both feet off the ground at once is a genuine feat.
activities.why: Jumping with both feet leaving the ground simultaneously, usually mastered around this age, requires a coordinated push-off and landing that's more demanding than walking or running. Simple balance challenges — a taped line, a low curb — build the core strength and body control that support all later sports and play.
steps: Model a small jump yourself and invite them to copy it — over a taped line or a low cushion.
Try a simple balance line (masking tape on the floor) to walk along, arms out for balance.
Keep sessions short and playful — a few tries at a time, spread across the day.
activities.parentNote: "The first real jump — both feet, off the ground, together — usually comes with a look of total shock on their own face. Cheer loudly. They'll do it forty more times in a row."
watchFor: Both feet leaving the ground briefly — a genuine gross motor milestone
Walking along a line with arms out for balance — core stability improving
Significant, persistent clumsiness or frequent falling by 30 months — worth mentioning at a check-up

label: 27–30 months
title: Growing independence
note: Pronoun mix-ups — using ‘you’ to mean ‘me’ — are typical at this stage and self-correct with time and modelling, not correction.

guide.try: Push the tricycle to get the pedalling motion going, then let her take over gradually. Many children need a few weeks of practice before it clicks.
guide.watch: She pushes the pedals with some coordination, even if inconsistent.
guide.see: No interest in or attempt at pedalling by 30 months.

milestones.text: Stands briefly on one foot with support

guide.try: Hold her hand and encourage a brief one-foot balance during play — ‘can you stand like a flamingo?’
guide.watch: She balances on one foot for a second or two with your hand for support.
guide.see: No attempt at single-leg balance, even supported, by 30 months.

milestones.text: Draws vertical and horizontal lines intentionally

guide.try: Draw a line slowly while she watches, then hand her the crayon and let her copy.
guide.watch: She produces a deliberate straight-ish line, not just random scribbles.
guide.see: No intentional line drawing at all by 30 months.

milestones.text: Uses simple pronouns, even if mixed up

guide.try: Model correct pronoun use naturally in your replies rather than correcting directly — the mix-ups resolve with exposure over time.
guide.watch: Pronouns like ‘I’, ‘me’, ‘you’ appearing in speech, even inconsistently.
guide.see: No pronoun use or self-reference at all by 30 months.

milestones.text: Speaks in 3-word phrases some of the time

guide.try: Expand her 2-word phrases into 3 — if she says ‘more juice’, respond ‘yes, more apple juice.’
guide.watch: Occasional 3-word combinations appearing spontaneously alongside 2-word ones.
guide.see: No 2-word combinations at all by 30 months warrants a speech evaluation.

guide.try: Ask simple questions during play and daily routines — ‘where's your shoe?’ — and give her time to respond.
guide.watch: She responds appropriately to simple ‘where’ questions, even without full sentences.
guide.see: No response to simple questions at all by 30 months.

milestones.text: Takes turns with heavy adult support

guide.try: Use a visual or physical marker — a timer, a token — to make an abstract concept like ‘turns’ concrete and predictable.
guide.watch: She waits for her turn with active adult guidance, without major distress.
guide.see: No capacity for turn-taking even with significant support by 30 months.

milestones.text: Shows early signs of sharing, even briefly

guide.try: Model sharing yourself — ‘here, you can have some of mine’ — and praise any spontaneous sharing warmly.
guide.watch: She offers a toy or object to another child or adult, even momentarily.
guide.see: This develops gradually through the preschool years — not a standalone concern yet.

milestones.text: Separates from a caregiver with less distress in familiar settings

guide.try: Practise short, predictable separations in familiar places — always saying goodbye clearly rather than sneaking away.
guide.watch: She settles within a few minutes after a caregiver leaves in a known, safe setting.
guide.see: Extreme, prolonged distress at every separation by 30 months is worth discussing.

relatedActivities.name: Turn-taking games
relatedActivities.tagline: Rolling a ball back and forth is early diplomacy training.

guide.try: Sort toys, laundry, or snacks by colour together, narrating as you go: ‘red ones here, blue ones there.’
guide.watch: She consistently groups objects by colour with minimal prompting.
guide.see: No sorting by any attribute at all by 30 months.

guide.try: Use size words constantly in daily life — ‘big spoon, small spoon’ — and let her point to which is which.
guide.watch: She correctly identifies the bigger or smaller of two objects when asked.
guide.see: No understanding of size concepts at all by 30 months.

milestones.text: Matches an object to its picture in a book

guide.try: Hold up a real object next to its picture in a book and ask ‘do these match?’
guide.watch: She connects a real object to its matching picture with reasonable consistency.
guide.see: No object-to-picture matching at all by 30 months.

relatedActivities.name: Counting everywhere
relatedActivities.tagline: Counting stairs is a math lesson that doesn't feel like one.

activityIntro.headline: Counting, colors, and the first taste of cooperating.
activityIntro.desc: Counting starts to become meaningful rather than a memorized recitation, color names click into place, and short stretches of cooperative play (rolling a ball back and forth, taking brief turns) begin to appear alongside the parallel play of earlier months. Many families begin exploring potty readiness around now.
activityIntro.note: Potty-training readiness varies enormously by child — physical, communicative, and interest cues matter more than a calendar date. There is no prize for starting early.

activities.name: Counting everywhere
activities.tagline: Counting stairs is a math lesson that doesn't feel like one.
activities.why: Reciting numbers in order comes before truly understanding quantity (that "three" means exactly three things), and this quarter is often when the two start to connect. Counting real objects — stairs, snacks, toys — repeatedly and out loud builds the concept of one-to-one correspondence that underlies all later math.
steps: Count aloud together during everyday moments — climbing stairs, placing snacks on a plate, putting toys away.
Point to each object as you say its number, slowly, to reinforce one-to-one matching.
Keep the numbers small (1–3, then up to 5) rather than rushing toward reciting to ten.
activities.parentNote: "Counting the same three stairs every single day sounds repetitive to you. To them, it's still a small thrilling discovery each time."
watchFor: Pointing to each object while counting — one-to-one correspondence developing
Correctly answering "how many?" for one or two objects — early number sense
No interest in numbers or counting by 3 years — worth mentioning at a check-up

activities.name: Turn-taking games
activities.tagline: Rolling a ball back and forth is early diplomacy training.
activities.why: Genuine turn-taking — waiting for your turn, handing something over, playing a simple back-and-forth game — is one of the building blocks of cooperative play with peers, which typically doesn't fully bloom until closer to age 3–4. Short, structured turn-taking games give safe practice for a skill that's still forming.
steps: Start with simple games with an obvious turn structure — rolling a ball back and forth, stacking one block each.
Narrate the turns explicitly: "My turn... now your turn."
Keep sessions brief and end on a positive note, before frustration builds.
activities.parentNote: "Waiting for a turn is genuinely one of the hardest things we ask of a two-year-old. A few successful rounds a day is real progress, not a small thing."
watchFor: Waiting even briefly for their turn without prompting — self-control developing
Handing an object over willingly during play — cooperative behavior forming
Extreme distress at any waiting at all past 3 years — worth discussing with your pediatrician

activities.name: Self-help skills practice
activities.tagline: Washing their own hands is a small ritual with a big payoff.
activities.why: Skills like washing hands, undressing, and pouring from a small jug build fine motor control and sequencing (remembering the steps in order). They also lay important groundwork for the independence that potty training and preschool routines will soon ask for.
steps: Provide a stable step stool at the sink so they can reach independently.
Break tasks into small, narrated steps: "Wet hands. Soap. Rub, rub, rub. Rinse."
Let them practice pouring water between two small cups over a tray, for controlled mess.
activities.parentNote: "A puddle on the bathroom floor from an enthusiastic hand-wash is evidence of a skill being built, not a mess to be annoyed about."
watchFor: Completing a familiar routine's steps with less help — sequencing improving
Showing interest in the toilet or potty — a possible readiness sign, not a requirement
Strong resistance to any self-help attempts at all — worth a gentler, no-pressure approach

label: 30–33 months
title: Question asker
note: The ‘why’ stage often peaks around now — it's genuine cognitive engagement with cause and reason, not a habit to discourage.

milestones.text: Jumps off a low step with both feet

guide.try: Hold her hand for the first few attempts from a very low step, then let her try independently as confidence builds.
guide.watch: Both feet leave and land together, even if the landing is a bit wobbly.
guide.see: No jumping attempts of any kind by 33 months.

milestones.text: Throws a ball overhand with improving aim

guide.try: Set up a simple target and take turns throwing toward it. Praise the effort and direction, not just accuracy.
guide.watch: She throws with a clear overhand motion and some intentional direction.
guide.see: Very limited throwing coordination by 33 months.

milestones.text: Copies a simple vertical line or circular shape

guide.try: Draw a line or rough circle slowly while narrating, then hand her the crayon to copy.
guide.watch: A recognisable attempt at the shape, even if imperfect.
guide.see: No intentional shape-copying attempts at all by 33 months.

relatedActivities.name: Drawing shapes and scissors practice
relatedActivities.tagline: A wobbly circle today, a written name in a couple of years.

guide.try: Expand every phrase she offers into a fuller sentence — if she says ‘more juice’, respond ‘yes, more apple juice, please.’
guide.watch: 3–4 word sentences appearing regularly and spontaneously in conversation.
guide.see: Fewer than 50 words or no phrase combinations by 30 months warrants a speech evaluation.

guide.try: Answer every ‘why?’ simply and seriously — ‘because the leaves need water to grow.’ This questioning is exactly the cognitive work she should be doing.
guide.watch: Persistent, genuine questioning about cause and reason.
guide.see: This is healthy — not a concern at all.

guide.try: Build two-step instructions into daily routines — ‘wash your hands, then sit down’ — without repeating.
guide.watch: She completes both steps in order without needing a reminder partway through.
guide.see: Not following even one-step instructions by 33 months.

milestones.text: Engages in short cooperative play with a peer

guide.try: Set up simple joint activities — a shared puzzle, building a tower together — with light adult guidance.
guide.watch: She plays with another child toward a shared goal for several minutes.
guide.see: No interest in or engagement with peer play by 33 months.

milestones.text: Shows early guilt or concern after doing something wrong

guide.try: When something goes wrong, focus on repair, not punishment: ‘that hurt your friend — what can we do to help?’
guide.watch: She looks away, apologises, or tries to make amends after a mistake.
guide.see: Not a standalone concern; a complete absence is worth mentioning at a check-up.

milestones.text: Names at least a few basic emotions in self or others

guide.try: Narrate emotions in the moment and in books — ‘that made you sad’, ‘the bear in the story looks scared.’
guide.watch: She uses or responds to a few emotion words like happy, sad, or mad.
guide.see: No emotional vocabulary at all by 33 months.

guide.try: Count through daily routines beyond 3 — stairs, snacks, toys — consistently and out loud.
guide.watch: She recites ‘1 to 5’ in order, even without matching it to objects yet.
guide.see: Cannot count to 3 in any form by 33 months.

guide.try: Sort a mixed pile of shapes together, narrating as you go: ‘circles here, squares there.’
guide.watch: She consistently groups objects by shape with minimal prompting.
guide.see: No sorting by any attribute at all by 33 months.

guide.try: Extend her pretend scenarios with a sequence — ‘feed the doll, now she's sleepy, let's tuck her in.’
guide.watch: She strings together two or more related pretend actions in order.
guide.see: No pretend or symbolic play at all by 33 months warrants evaluation.

relatedActivities.name: Why questions welcome
relatedActivities.tagline: "Why is the sky blue?" deserves a real answer, at their level.

activityIntro.headline: Complex sentences, and the first flickers of empathy.
activityIntro.desc: Four- and five-word sentences become common, "why" questions arrive in force, and the first genuine signs of empathy — comforting a crying friend, offering a toy unprompted — start to show up. Fine motor skills sharpen too: circles get drawn, and scissors (with supervision) become fascinating.
activityIntro.note: The endless "why" questions can be exhausting, but they're a sign of genuinely sophisticated causal reasoning developing — one of the more remarkable cognitive leaps of toddlerhood.

activities.name: Why questions welcome
activities.tagline: "Why is the sky blue?" deserves a real answer, at their level.
activities.why: The flood of "why" questions at this age reflects a genuine leap in causal reasoning — understanding that things happen for reasons, and wanting to know what those reasons are. Answering seriously, even with simple explanations, reinforces curiosity as a rewarded, safe behavior rather than an annoyance to be shut down.
steps: Give a short, honest, age-appropriate answer rather than "just because."
When you don't know, say so and, if possible, look it up together — modeling curiosity as a lifelong habit.
If the questions become circular or overwhelming, it's fine to say "Let's think about that one later" — boundaries are healthy too.
activities.parentNote: "By question forty of the day, patience runs thin for everyone. It's alright to set a limit. It doesn't undo the curiosity you've already nurtured."
watchFor: Follow-up questions building on your answer — genuine reasoning at work
Applying an explanation to a new, similar situation — early transfer of learning
Little curiosity or question-asking at all by 3 years — worth mentioning at a check-up

activities.name: Comfort the teddy
activities.tagline: Practicing kindness on a stuffed animal first.
activities.why: Around this age, children begin to show early empathy — noticing when someone is upset and attempting to help, even clumsily. Role-playing caregiving with a doll or stuffed animal ("Teddy fell down, is he okay?") gives safe, low-stakes practice for a skill that will matter enormously in real friendships.
steps: Set up a small scenario: "Oh no, teddy bumped his head. What should we do?"
Model comforting language and actions — a gentle pat, "It's okay, teddy" — for them to copy.
Point out real moments of empathy when they happen naturally: "You noticed your friend was sad. That was kind."
activities.parentNote: "The first time they unprompted pat a crying sibling and say 'it's okay,' something in you will melt a little. Let it."
watchFor: Comforting a toy or person without being asked — spontaneous empathy
Noticing others' emotions and naming them — social awareness growing
No response at all to others' distress by age 3 — worth discussing with your pediatrician

activities.name: Drawing shapes and scissors practice
activities.tagline: A wobbly circle today, a written name in a couple of years.
activities.why: Drawing a rough circle and beginning to use child-safe scissors are both key pre-writing skills, building the hand strength, grip, and bilateral coordination (one hand holding paper, the other cutting) needed for later handwriting. This should always be closely supervised at this age.
steps: Demonstrate drawing a circle slowly, then let them try to copy it freehand.
Offer blunt-tipped, child-safe scissors and strips of paper to snip, with close supervision throughout.
Praise the attempt and control shown, not just the end result — a messy circle is a genuine achievement here.
activities.parentNote: "Their circle will look more like a spiral. That's exactly the right amount of circle for right now."
watchFor: A closed, roughly circular shape — a genuine fine motor milestone
Opening and closing scissors with some control, even if not cutting cleanly — grip strength developing
Strong avoidance of any drawing or cutting tools by 3 years — worth mentioning at a check-up

label: 33–36 months
title: Nearly three
note: These months are the direct bridge into the 3–4 year stage that follows — a wide range of readiness at the three-year mark is completely normal.

milestones.text: Walks up stairs alternating feet independently

guide.try: Let her practise on real stairs with a rail, without holding your hand every time, when it's safe to do so.
guide.watch: One foot per step going up, with little to no need for support.
guide.see: Still cannot navigate stairs at all by 3 years.

guide.try: Give her time to attempt undressing even if it's slow. Start with easy items — socks, loose shoes — before fastened clothing.
guide.watch: Removing socks, shoes, or a loose top independently with some effort.
guide.see: No attempt at self-undressing by 3 years.

milestones.text: Copies a circle with a crayon

guide.try: Draw a large circle slowly while she watches, narrating the motion, then let her try on her own.
guide.watch: A roughly closed curved line — it doesn't need to be a perfect circle.
guide.see: No intentional drawing or mark-making by 3 years.

milestones.text: Speaks in 3–4 word sentences understood by familiar adults

guide.try: Have real back-and-forth conversations throughout the day, not just instructions — ask her real questions and wait for real answers.
guide.watch: Sentences understood by you and other familiar caregivers most of the time.
guide.see: Fewer than 50 words or no phrase combinations by 3 years warrants a speech evaluation.

guide.try: Model correct pronoun use naturally in your replies — mix-ups are common and self-correct with exposure.
guide.watch: Pronouns appearing in speech, even if mixed up at first.
guide.see: No pronoun use or consistent self-reference by 3 years.

guide.try: Give multi-step instructions in daily routines — ‘get your shoes, put them on, then come here’ — building from two steps to three.
guide.watch: She completes multi-step instructions in the right order without reminders.
guide.see: Not following two-step instructions by 3 years.

milestones.text: Begins genuine cooperative play toward a shared goal

guide.try: Set up simple joint activities — building a tower together, a team puzzle — and narrate taking turns.
guide.watch: She plays with another child toward a shared outcome, even briefly.
guide.see: No interest in other children or cooperative activity by 3 years.

milestones.text: Shows a wide, clear range of emotional expression

guide.try: Name emotions as they happen — ‘you look really frustrated right now, that's hard.’ This labelling is the first step toward regulation.
guide.watch: Clear expression of happiness, anger, sadness, fear, and excitement.
guide.see: Very flat affect or only one or two emotional expressions by 3 years.

milestones.text: Shows real interest in other children as playmates

guide.try: Arrange regular contact with same-age children — playgroups, park time. Watching before joining in is completely fine.
guide.watch: She notices, watches, and approaches other children with curiosity.
guide.see: Complete indifference to or avoidance of other children by 3 years.

relatedActivities.name: Calm-down corner practice
relatedActivities.tagline: Teach the strategy on a good day, so it's there on a hard one.

relatedActivities.name: Playdate practice
relatedActivities.tagline: The training ground for preschool starts here.

guide.try: Name colours constantly in daily life — ‘your red shirt, the blue sky’ — without quizzing, just narrating.
guide.watch: She correctly names 2–3 colours when asked.
guide.see: No colour recognition at all by 3 years.

milestones.text: Counts up to 3 objects with one-to-one correspondence

guide.try: Count real objects together — grapes, stairs, toys — touching each one as you say its number.
guide.watch: She touches each object once while counting up to 3 accurately.
guide.see: No counting or one-to-one correspondence at all by 3 years.

milestones.text: Engages in rich make-believe play with multiple steps

guide.try: Join her pretend play and extend it — if she's cooking, ask what's for dinner and where the plates are.
guide.watch: She creates a multi-step imaginative scenario with dolls, animals, or everyday objects.
guide.see: No pretend or symbolic play at all by 3 years warrants evaluation.

activityIntro.headline: Almost three. Ready for the next adventure.
activityIntro.desc: The final quarter before the third birthday often brings clearer speech (strangers can understand most of it), early rhyming and letter awareness, more genuine interest in playing alongside — and sometimes with — other children, and the beginnings of self-regulation strategies your toddler can use on their own.
activityIntro.note: Take a moment here. Three years ago you were doing skin-to-skin holds with someone who couldn't lift their own head. Now you're teaching them to calm themselves down, ask "why," and comfort a friend. That is not a small thing.

activities.name: Rhyme time
activities.tagline: Playing with sounds now is reading practice for later.
activities.why: Noticing and enjoying rhymes builds phonological awareness — the ability to hear and play with the sound structure of language — one of the strongest predictors of later reading success. Nursery rhymes, silly made-up rhymes, and songs all build this skill through pure play, with no formal instruction needed yet.
steps: Sing and recite familiar nursery rhymes often, leaving off the last rhyming word for them to fill in.
Play silly rhyming games with their own name or favorite objects: "Cat, hat, bat, sat!"
Clap out syllables in simple words together as an early rhythm and sound-awareness game.
activities.parentNote: "Nonsense rhymes that make no sense at all are still doing real work. The sillier, the more likely they'll ask to repeat it — and repetition is the whole mechanism."
watchFor: Filling in a missing rhyming word from a familiar song — phonological awareness developing
Making up their own (often nonsensical) rhymes — playing with sound patterns
No enjoyment of songs, rhymes, or rhythm at all by age 3 — worth mentioning at a check-up

activities.name: Calm-down corner practice
activities.tagline: Teach the strategy on a good day, so it's there on a hard one.
activities.why: By nearly three, many toddlers can start learning simple, concrete self-regulation strategies — a few deep breaths, squeezing a soft toy, sitting in a cozy designated spot — though they'll still need your calm presence and coaching most of the time. Introducing this proactively, not just during a meltdown, makes it far more likely to actually help in the moment.
steps: Set up a small, cozy "calm corner" with a cushion, a soft toy, and maybe a picture book — introduce it during a calm moment, not a crisis.
Practice a simple breathing cue together when everyone's relaxed: "Smell the flower, blow out the candle."
During an actual upset, gently offer the corner and the breathing cue as an option, without forcing it.
activities.parentNote: "This will not work the first ten times you offer it during an actual meltdown. It's still worth building — the skill is being installed for later, even when today's tantrum doesn't show it."
watchFor: Going to the calm corner unprompted when upset — a strategy taking hold
Copying the breathing cue during a mildly frustrating moment — early self-regulation
Escalating distress at every offer of the strategy — pause it and revisit in a few weeks

activities.name: Playdate practice
activities.tagline: The training ground for preschool starts here.
activities.why: Cooperative play with peers — genuinely playing together rather than alongside — starts to take root closer to age 3, though it develops unevenly and needs adult support for a while yet. Structured, short playdates give practice sharing, negotiating, and navigating small conflicts in a supported setting before starting preschool.
steps: Keep early playdates short (30–45 minutes) with a simple, shared activity like building blocks or a sandbox.
Stay nearby to help narrate and referee small conflicts: "You both want the truck. Let's take turns."
End on a high note, before fatigue or overstimulation turns things sour.
activities.parentNote: "It will look chaotic and a little tense at times. That's what learning to share a space with another small, opinionated person actually looks like from the outside."
watchFor: Brief cooperative play — building or role-playing together — real progress
Verbal negotiation attempts ("my turn," "you can have it after") — social skills developing
Consistent extreme distress around any peer interaction — worth discussing with your pediatrician

label: 3–4 years
title: Creative thinker
note: Imaginative play is a sign of healthy development — resist jumping in to ‘fix’ their stories. Let fantasy unfold.

guide.try: Make it a game — hop to the door, hop 3 times. Many children master hopping closer to 4 years, so stay patient.
guide.watch: At least 1–2 hops on each foot without putting the other foot down.
guide.see: Still unable to stand briefly on one foot by 4 years.

guide.try: Push her tricycle to get the sensation going, then let her take over. Most children need a few weeks of practice before the pedalling motion clicks.
guide.watch: She pedals smoothly and steers with reasonable control.
guide.see: Very limited leg coordination or strength by 4 years.

guide.try: Use child-safe scissors on playdough first before paper — it requires the same motion with less frustration. Hold the paper steady while she cuts.
guide.watch: She opens and closes scissors and makes snips in paper with support.
guide.see: No fine motor control with tools by 4 years.

milestones.text: Draws a person with 2 body parts

guide.try: Draw a simple person together and name the parts as you add them. Ask her to add a part: ‘What else does a person have?’
guide.watch: She draws a head with at least one other feature (eyes, legs, arms).
guide.see: No representational drawing by 4 years.

guide.try: Build dressing time into the routine — give her extra time so she can do it herself. Start with large buttons and elastic waists.
guide.watch: She puts on most items independently and asks for help only with complex fastenings.
guide.see: No attempt at independent dressing by 4 years.

guide.try: Engage in real conversations at mealtimes without screens. Ask open questions: ‘What was the best part of your day?’ rather than yes/no questions.
guide.watch: Consistent 4–6 word sentences with correct grammar most of the time.
guide.see: Sentences still mostly 2 words at 3.5 years warrants a speech assessment.

guide.try: Ask her to tell you about something that happened. Recap events together in sequence: ‘First we did… then we…’
guide.watch: She narrates a sequence of two or more events in order.
guide.see: No ability to retell a recent event by 4 years.

guide.try: Answer all questions genuinely and simply, even repetitive ones. These questions show she is building causal and temporal understanding.
guide.watch: A variety of question types — who, what, where, when, how — alongside ‘why?’
guide.see: No questioning at all by 3.5 years.

guide.try: Compare things together constantly: ‘This apple is red. Is this one the same colour or different?’ Make it a natural part of play.
guide.watch: She correctly identifies matching pairs and spots differences between objects.
guide.see: No concept of same/different by 4 years.

guide.try: Facilitate regular playdates with the same children so friendships can form. Talk about her friends by name and show genuine interest in them.
guide.watch: She mentions specific children by name and asks to see them.
guide.see: Complete disinterest in peer relationships by 4 years.

guide.try: Board games designed for this age are excellent — simple memory games, Snakes & Ladders. Rules give structure to turn-taking.
guide.watch: She waits her turn with only occasional reminders.
guide.see: Unable to take turns even with significant support by 4 years.

guide.try: Play simple rule-based games together every day. Explain rules slowly and be consistent — she is learning that rules apply to everyone, including you.
guide.watch: She follows game rules and appeals to rules when others break them.
guide.see: No understanding of rules or structured play by 4 years.

guide.try: When she does something wrong, focus on the behaviour not the person: ‘That hurt your friend. What can we do to help?’ Repair matters more than punishment.
guide.watch: She looks away, lowers her head, or spontaneously tries to make amends.
guide.see: Absence of any guilt response may be worth noting at your check-up.

relatedActivities.name: Naming the feeling
relatedActivities.tagline: "You're frustrated" lands very differently than "stop crying."

relatedActivities.name: Simple pretend errands
relatedActivities.tagline: Playing "shop" is secretly a lesson in taking turns.

guide.try: Count through daily routines — stairs, grapes, steps to the door. The sequence needs to be heard hundreds of times before it becomes reliable.
guide.watch: She counts to 10 in sequence with one-to-one correspondence to objects.
guide.see: Cannot count to 5 by 4 years.

milestones.text: Knows first and last name / age

guide.try: Ask her name and age regularly in a fun way: ‘Excuse me, what is your name?’ Practise with relatives so it becomes natural.
guide.watch: She answers with her full name and age (or holds up correct fingers) reliably.
guide.see: Cannot state her first name by 4 years.

guide.try: Use time words consistently: ‘Yesterday we went to the park. Today we are at home. Tomorrow is Sunday.’ A simple picture calendar makes this tangible.
guide.watch: She uses yesterday, today, and tomorrow approximately correctly.
guide.see: No time concept vocabulary by 4 years.

guide.try: Draw shapes together and name them. Playdough rolling into circles and snakes is great pre-drawing practice.
guide.watch: Circles, crosses, and rough squares appearing in her drawings.
guide.see: No intentional shapes in drawing by 4 years.

relatedActivities.name: Story-powered pretend play
relatedActivities.tagline: A cardboard box is only a box until someone believes it's a spaceship.

relatedActivities.name: Kitchen helper tasks
relatedActivities.tagline: Pouring, stirring, and tearing lettuce are real jobs, not busywork.

relatedActivities.name: Counting real things
relatedActivities.tagline: Counting stairs matters more than reciting numbers in a row.

activityIntro.headline: The imagination turns on.
activityIntro.desc: Somewhere in the fourth year, pretend play stops being simple repetition and becomes genuine storytelling — a spoon becomes a phone, a blanket becomes a cave, and your child starts narrating a world only they can see. Alongside it comes an intense drive toward independence ("I do it myself") and, often, big feelings that outpace the words to express them. Both are signs of a rapidly developing mind, not a harder child.
activityIntro.note: The intensity of tantrums often peaks around this age precisely because language and self-awareness are growing faster than emotional regulation. This is a temporary gap, not a character trait.

activities.name: Story-powered pretend play
activities.tagline: A cardboard box is only a box until someone believes it's a spaceship.
activities.why: Symbolic pretend play — using one object to represent another — is one of the clearest markers of advancing cognitive development at this age. It builds narrative thinking, flexible problem-solving, and the ability to hold someone else's perspective in mind, since good pretend play usually involves a character who isn't you. The stories your child tells are early drafts of how they'll later organise real experience into language.
steps: Follow their lead rather than directing the plot. If the spoon is a phone, answer it when it rings.
Offer open-ended props — boxes, scarves, blocks — over single-purpose toys. A box can be anything; a toy phone can only be a phone.
Ask "what happens next?" instead of suggesting an ending. Let them author the story, even if it makes no logical sense.
activities.parentNote: "You don't need a script or a costume box. You need fifteen minutes and a willingness to take a pretend phone call seriously. That's the whole activity."
watchFor: Objects standing in for other objects — symbolic thinking in action
Simple story arcs with a beginning and an end — narrative structure forming
Little to no pretend play by age 4 — worth mentioning at a check-up

activities.name: Naming the feeling
activities.tagline: "You're frustrated" lands very differently than "stop crying."
activities.why: A three-year-old's emotions frequently arrive faster than their vocabulary for them. Giving a feeling a name — "you seem frustrated that the tower fell" — is one of the most consistently supported tools in early emotional development, because naming an emotion measurably reduces its intensity by engaging the thinking brain alongside the feeling brain. You're not fixing the feeling. You're giving it a shape they can hold.
steps: When big emotion shows up, name it calmly and without judgement: "You're really angry right now."
Resist jumping straight to solving or correcting. Let the naming sit for a moment first.
In calm moments, build a wider feelings vocabulary through books and simple face-and-word games — disappointed, proud, nervous, silly.
activities.parentNote: "You will name a feeling and still get the tantrum. That's normal — naming isn't a magic off-switch. It's a skill they're borrowing from you now so they can use it themselves later."
watchFor: Occasionally naming their own feelings unprompted — real progress
Faster recovery from big emotions over time — regulation strengthening
Extreme, prolonged meltdowns most of the day, most days — worth discussing with your pediatrician

activities.name: Simple pretend errands
activities.tagline: Playing "shop" is secretly a lesson in taking turns.
activities.why: Around this age, play with another child starts shifting from side-by-side (parallel play) toward genuine back-and-forth. Simple role scenarios with clear turns — shopkeeper and customer, doctor and patient — give structure to that shift, making turn-taking and shared rules concrete rather than abstract.
steps: Set up a simple two-role scenario with household items — a "shop" of pantry items, a pretend doctor's kit.
Model one round yourself, narrating the turn-taking out loud: "My turn to buy, then your turn."
Let the roles swap naturally, stepping back once they've got the rhythm.
activities.parentNote: "If it dissolves into an argument over who's the shopkeeper, that's not a failed activity — that's the actual lesson happening in real time."
watchFor: Waiting for a turn without a prompt — self-regulation developing
Assigning roles to others — early social planning
Persistent difficulty playing alongside any other child — worth mentioning to a teacher or pediatrician

activities.name: Dressing themselves, however long it takes
activities.tagline: The shirt on backwards is a win, not a mistake.
activities.why: Self-dressing is one of the clearest, most repeatable ways a three-year-old practices sequencing, problem-solving, and frustration tolerance simultaneously. Every fumbled sleeve is genuine cognitive and motor work — figuring out which hole goes where builds the same planning skills used later for far more complex tasks.
steps: Build in extra time — five slow minutes of them dressing beats ninety rushed seconds of you doing it for them.
Lay clothes out front-facing so the logic is visible; offer two outfit choices rather than open-ended selection.
Help only when asked, or when frustration is clearly rising past the productive kind.
activities.parentNote: "Let them leave the house with the shirt on backwards sometimes. Nobody in the history of preschool has ever been harmed by a backwards shirt, and the confidence they build fixing it themselves next time is worth far more."
watchFor: Managing simple fasteners like Velcro or large buttons — fine motor progress
Trying before asking for help — independence taking hold
Complete refusal to attempt any self-dressing by age 4 — worth a gentle conversation with their pediatrician

activities.name: Kitchen helper tasks
activities.tagline: Pouring, stirring, and tearing lettuce are real jobs, not busywork.
activities.why: Practical, real-world tasks with a genuine purpose — not toy versions of adult work — build fine motor control, sequencing, and a felt sense of contribution that praise alone can't replicate. A child who stirred the batter feels differently about the finished cake than one who was simply served it.
steps: Give them one real, contained task: pouring a measured cup of flour, washing vegetables, tearing lettuce for salad.
Break instructions into single, simple steps rather than a full recipe's worth at once.
Expect spills. A tray or towel underneath turns mess into a non-issue rather than a source of tension.
activities.parentNote: "It will take three times as long and the kitchen will need a wipe-down after. That's the cost of a genuinely useful skill being built, not a sign something went wrong."
watchFor: Following a two-step instruction in order — sequencing developing
Pride in showing others what they made — early sense of contribution
Persistent difficulty with any pouring or tearing motion — worth a quick fine-motor check-in

activities.name: Counting real things
activities.tagline: Counting stairs matters more than reciting numbers in a row.
activities.why: Reciting "one, two, three" is a memorised sequence. Touching each grape while saying its number — one-to-one correspondence — is the actual mathematical skill underneath counting, and it develops well after the rote sequence does. Real objects make the connection concrete in a way abstract counting can't.
steps: Count anything physical and touchable — stairs as you climb, grapes on a plate, socks from the laundry basket.
Touch or move each item as you say its number, keeping pace deliberately slow.
Ask "how many is that altogether?" afterward — this checks whether the last number said represents the total, a genuinely tricky concept at this age.
activities.parentNote: "If they recount and get a different number each time, that's completely typical, not a gap to worry about. The skill is still under construction."
watchFor: Touching each object once while counting it — one-to-one correspondence forming
Correctly answering "how many altogether" for small sets — cardinality emerging
No interest in or awareness of numbers by age 4 — worth mentioning at a check-up

label: 4–5 years
title: School ready
note: School readiness is about social skills as much as academic ones. Sharing, listening, and managing frustration matter most.

guide.try: Demonstrate skipping slowly and exaggeratedly. Most children master galloping first then skipping — both require alternating rhythmic movement.
guide.watch: She gallops with a consistent lead foot, or skips with alternating feet.
guide.see: Very limited gross motor coordination by 5 years.

guide.try: Start with a large ball bounced from close range. Move further back as she improves. Two hands together first, then progress to one-handed.
guide.watch: She catches a bounced ball consistently with both hands from a metre away.
guide.see: No ball-catching ability by 5 years alongside other motor concerns.

guide.try: Write her name in dots for her to trace first. Use sandbox writing, finger paint, and chalk on pavements — gross motor versions of letter formation build the skill faster.
guide.watch: All letters of her name written in order, legibly enough to be recognisable.
guide.see: No letter formation at all by 5 years.

guide.try: Model correct grip at every meal and gently correct hold when needed. Thicker-handled cutlery is easier for small hands to grip.
guide.watch: She scoops and spears food reliably with a fork and spoon.
guide.see: Very limited utensil use by 5 years.

guide.try: Practise with larger buttons first — dress-up clothes are ideal. A button frame (two pieces of fabric) lets her practise without the awkward angle of clothing on her own body.
guide.watch: She manages large buttons and a zip pull independently.
guide.see: Very limited fine motor development overall by 5 years.

guide.try: Encourage her to order her own food, greet people, and answer simple questions from familiar adults. The more practice, the clearer her speech becomes.
guide.watch: Unfamiliar people understand her most of the time without you needing to translate.
guide.see: Strangers understand less than half of what she says by 4 years — seek a speech assessment.

guide.try: Ask about her day at a fixed time: ‘Tell me three things that happened today.’ Structure it the same way each time so she knows what to expect.
guide.watch: She narrates events in chronological order with beginning, middle, and end.
guide.see: Cannot sequence or retell events by 5 years.

milestones.text: Understands most of what adults say

guide.try: Engage in full adult conversations in her presence. Include her — explain things genuinely rather than simplifying excessively.
guide.watch: She follows conversations, responds appropriately, and asks relevant questions.
guide.see: Frequent misunderstandings or failure to follow conversation by 5 years.

guide.try: Read rhyming books daily. Play ‘does that rhyme?’ games: ‘Cat and bat — do they rhyme?’ Make it playful.
guide.watch: She identifies when two words rhyme and can generate a rhyming word with prompting.
guide.see: No phonological awareness by 5 years needs assessment before school entry.

relatedActivities.name: Rhyme time
relatedActivities.tagline: Hearing that "cat" and "hat" sound alike comes before reading either word.

relatedActivities.name: Letter hunt
relatedActivities.tagline: The letters in their own name are the ones they'll learn first, and fastest.

relatedActivities.name: Retelling the story
relatedActivities.tagline: Asking "what happened first?" builds a skill they'll use in every subject later.

guide.try: Facilitate regular contact with the same children. Ask her about specific friends and remember their names — your interest signals that friendships matter.
guide.watch: She mentions specific friends by name and shows excitement about seeing them.
guide.see: No peer relationships at all by 5 years.

guide.try: Play board games with progressively complex rules. Involve her in reading the rules and explain why each rule exists.
guide.watch: She follows a 3–4 step rule set and reminds others of the rules.
guide.see: Cannot follow simple game rules by 5 years.

guide.try: Model emotional vocabulary constantly: ‘I feel frustrated when the traffic is bad. What do you feel when that happens?’ Feelings charts expand the vocabulary.
guide.watch: She labels her own feelings with words rather than acting them out physically.
guide.see: No emotional vocabulary or exclusively physical emotional expression by 5 years.

guide.try: When situations arise, name the concept: ‘You feel that was unfair because she got more. Let us make it equal.’ Real situations teach this better than abstract lessons.
guide.watch: She uses ‘fair’ and ‘unfair’ appropriately and applies the concept to others.
guide.see: No understanding of fairness or equity by 5 years.

relatedActivities.name: Simple board games
relatedActivities.tagline: Losing gracefully is a harder skill than winning gracefully.

relatedActivities.name: Being a good friend
relatedActivities.tagline: "How do you think that made her feel?" is a question worth asking often.

guide.try: Count everything in daily life beyond 10. The teen numbers (11–19) are linguistically irregular in English — they need more repetition than 1–10.
guide.watch: She counts to 20 in sequence with reliable one-to-one correspondence to at least 10 objects.
guide.see: Cannot count reliably to 10 by 5 years.

guide.try: Point out letters in the environment — shop signs, cereal boxes, books. Her own name is the best starting point for letter recognition.
guide.watch: She recognises most letters of the alphabet by sight and reads a few high-frequency words.
guide.see: No letter recognition by 5 years needs assessment before school.

guide.try: Use ordinal words in daily routines: ‘You are first in the bath today. Your brother is second.’ Physical race games reinforce this naturally.
guide.watch: She uses first, second, and third correctly in context.
guide.see: No sequencing or ordinal number concept by 5 years.

guide.try: Sort objects by two properties simultaneously: ‘Put the big red ones here, and the small red ones there.’ Sorting games make this concrete.
guide.watch: She can sort a group of objects by colour and size simultaneously.
guide.see: No sorting by single attribute by 5 years.

relatedActivities.name: Counting collections
relatedActivities.tagline: Sorting buttons by colour is quietly teaching more math than it looks like.

activityIntro.headline: Letters, numbers, and real friendships.
activityIntro.desc: This year, play starts running on rules instead of just imagination — board games, made-up games with their own logic, and friendships that involve genuine give-and-take. Letters and sounds start clicking together rather than sitting as separate facts, and counting starts meaning something rather than just sounding right. Attention spans stretch, though unevenly, and empathy takes real, visible steps forward.
activityIntro.note: "Readiness" for reading and writing varies enormously at this age — some children are decoding simple words, others are still learning letter sounds, and both are entirely on track. Comparing timelines between children rarely helps and often discourages.

activities.name: Rhyme time
activities.tagline: Hearing that "cat" and "hat" sound alike comes before reading either word.
activities.why: Phonological awareness — hearing and playing with the sound structure of language, separate from meaning — is one of the strongest known predictors of later reading ability. Rhyming is one of its simplest, earliest forms: noticing that two words share an ending sound. This is ear training, and it comes well before any letter on a page.
steps: Read rhyming books and pause before the rhyming word, letting them guess it.
Play silly rhyme games in the car or bath: "what rhymes with 'bed'? Zed, red, said..."
Nonsense rhymes count fully — "cat, mat, zat" — the sound pattern matters, not the dictionary.
activities.parentNote: "If real rhymes don't come yet, silly made-up ones are just as valuable. The skill being built is hearing the pattern, not knowing the word."
watchFor: Supplying the rhyming word before you finish the sentence — pattern recognition strong
Making up their own silly rhymes unprompted — real engagement with sound
No sense of rhyme at all by age 5 — worth mentioning to a teacher or pediatrician

activities.name: Letter hunt
activities.tagline: The letters in their own name are the ones they'll learn first, and fastest.
activities.why: Letter recognition tends to start with personally meaningful letters — the ones in a child's own name — before generalising outward. Turning letter-spotting into a hunt keeps it playful rather than drill-like, and grounds an abstract symbol system in the concrete, familiar world around them.
steps: Start with the letters in their name — spot them on cereal boxes, street signs, book covers.
Once those feel solid, widen the hunt to a "letter of the day" found anywhere you go.
Say the letter's sound alongside its name — "S, sss, like snake" — sound matters more than name for reading later.
activities.parentNote: "Their own name is the doorway into the whole alphabet. Start there and let their curiosity carry the rest — you don't need a workbook to make this stick."
watchFor: Recognising the letters of their own name reliably — a strong early milestone
Attempting to sound out simple, familiar words — decoding beginning
No interest in or recognition of any letters by age 5 — worth flagging with a teacher

activities.name: Retelling the story
activities.tagline: Asking "what happened first?" builds a skill they'll use in every subject later.
activities.why: Retelling a story in order — beginning, middle, end — draws on memory, sequencing, and comprehension all at once, and is a strong predictor of later reading comprehension specifically, beyond just enjoying a story being read. It's a genuinely different skill from listening, and it needs deliberate practice.
steps: After a familiar book, ask them to tell you the story in their own words, using the pictures as prompts if needed.
Ask sequencing questions: "what happened first? Then what? How did it end?"
Accept an imperfect retelling warmly — the goal is practicing structure, not reciting it exactly.
activities.parentNote: "They'll skip whole chunks and invent bits that never happened. That's not a failed retelling — that's a child actively rebuilding a story in their own mind, which is the entire point."
watchFor: Events told in roughly the right order — sequencing developing
Using story language like "once upon a time" or "the end" — narrative structure absorbed
Significant difficulty following or discussing simple stories by age 5 — worth a conversation with a teacher

activities.name: Counting collections
activities.tagline: Sorting buttons by colour is quietly teaching more math than it looks like.
activities.why: Sorting, grouping, and comparing quantities — "which pile has more?" — builds the number sense that underlies all later arithmetic, and it develops through hands-on comparison well before formal addition and subtraction make sense. A child who can confidently say which of two piles is bigger has real mathematical understanding, whether or not they know the word "more."
steps: Gather a small collection of similar objects — buttons, blocks, coins — and sort them by one property, like colour or size.
Count each group aloud, then ask which group has more and how they know.
Combine two small groups and count the new total together, connecting counting to combining.
activities.parentNote: "A tin of buttons does more for early math than most flashcards. Let them dump it out, sort it their own way, and count it three different times if they want to."
watchFor: Comparing two small groups correctly without counting — early number sense
Counting collections of 10 or more with reasonable accuracy — solid progress
No interest in comparing "more" and "less" by age 5 — worth mentioning to a teacher

activities.name: Simple board games
activities.tagline: Losing gracefully is a harder skill than winning gracefully.
activities.why: Simple rule-based games — snakes and ladders, matching games, simple card games — teach turn-taking, waiting, following shared rules, and, crucially, tolerating losing. These are executive-function skills, not just social niceties, and they're genuinely difficult at this age. Practicing them in the low-stakes context of a game is far gentler than learning them for the first time somewhere that matters more.
steps: Choose games with simple, visible rules and short rounds — long games test patience past its limit at this age.
Play by the real rules rather than always letting them win — occasional genuine loss is where the real learning happens.
Narrate your own feelings when you lose: "Oh, I lost that round. That's a bit disappointing. I'll try again."
activities.parentNote: "Losing well is not instinctive at five. It's modelled. Let them see you lose a game without falling apart, and eventually they'll borrow that response for themselves."
watchFor: Waiting for their turn without constant reminders — self-regulation strengthening
Recovering from a loss within a few minutes — emotional resilience building
Extreme distress at every loss that doesn't ease with practice — worth a gentle, ongoing focus rather than a red flag

activities.name: Being a good friend
activities.tagline: "How do you think that made her feel?" is a question worth asking often.
activities.why: Perspective-taking — genuinely imagining another person's experience, not just knowing right from wrong — develops substantially this year. Real conflicts with friends and siblings are the richest available material for practicing it, far more than any hypothetical lesson could be.
steps: When a real conflict happens, ask rather than tell: "how do you think that made them feel?"
Help them notice the other child's face and body, not just their own feelings in the moment.
Talk through repair together — what could be said or done now to help their friend feel better.
activities.parentNote: "They will get it wrong plenty of times before they get it right. Every one of those wrong guesses is still practice at the actual skill — imagining a mind that isn't their own."
watchFor: Correctly guessing how a friend feels in a real situation — perspective-taking developing
Offering comfort to an upset friend without being prompted — empathy in action
Persistent difficulty recognising others' feelings by age 5 — worth mentioning to a teacher

label: 5–6 years
title: Young learner
note: Children this age learn best through play. Reading together daily for 20 min has an outsized impact on literacy.

guide.try: Start with a balance bike if possible — children who master balance first learn to pedal a real bike much faster. Training wheels are a valid alternative.
guide.watch: She pedals, steers, and brakes with reasonable confidence on training wheels.
guide.see: Very limited gross motor coordination by 6 years.

guide.try: Tracing books, dot-to-dot activities, and writing through sand or rice trays all build letter formation. Correct pencil grip now saves remediation later.
guide.watch: She copies all letters in her name and basic shapes (triangle, rectangle, diamond).
guide.see: Cannot copy simple shapes or letters by 6 years — an occupational therapy assessment may help.

guide.try: Use a lacing board or large practice shoe first. The ‘bunny ears’ method is often easier for young children than the traditional loop-swoop-pull.
guide.watch: She can make the first knot and attempt a bow, even with some help.
guide.see: Not a major concern if other fine motor skills are developing — laces are genuinely hard.

guide.try: Progress from a large soft ball to a smaller ball over weeks. Catching accuracy improves dramatically with repeated practice rather than instruction.
guide.watch: She catches a tennis-sized ball thrown from 2 metres away more often than not.
guide.see: Cannot catch any ball by 6 years alongside other motor concerns.

guide.try: Ensure at least 60 minutes of active play every day. Unstructured outdoor play naturally includes running, jumping, climbing, and balancing.
guide.watch: She seeks out active play enthusiastically and keeps up physically with peers.
guide.see: Consistent avoidance of physical activity or significant physical fatigue by 6 years.

relatedActivities.name: Writing their name
relatedActivities.tagline: Legible, not perfect, is the actual goal at this age.

guide.try: Ask her to make up a story and you write it down exactly as she tells it. This validates her narrative voice and improves both length and complexity.
guide.watch: Stories with a character, a problem, and a resolution appearing naturally.
guide.see: Cannot construct a simple narrative by 6 years.

guide.try: Talk about tomorrow and next week regularly: ‘On Saturday we will go to the market. What do you think we will find?’ Future planning in conversation builds tense.
guide.watch: Consistent and correct use of will/going to forms in natural speech.
guide.see: Major grammatical errors (wrong tense in all sentences) persisting at 6 years.

guide.try: Read together every single day for at least 20 minutes. Point to words as you read. Decodable phonics readers are designed to build this skill step by step.
guide.watch: She reads simple CVC words (cat, dog, sit) and high-frequency sight words reliably.
guide.see: No phonics awareness or word reading by 6 years needs a literacy assessment.

guide.try: Have genuine two-way conversations at meals. Ask her opinions and respond seriously. Mealtime conversation is one of the strongest predictors of vocabulary development.
guide.watch: She initiates conversation, stays on topic, and asks and answers questions naturally.
guide.see: Very limited conversational reciprocity by 6 years.

relatedActivities.name: Sound blending games
relatedActivities.tagline: "C... a... t" becoming "cat" out loud is the moment reading clicks.

relatedActivities.name: Retell in order
relatedActivities.tagline: Beginning, middle, end — the same shape as every essay they'll ever write.

guide.try: Talk about movies and stories: ‘Is that a real dinosaur or a pretend one? How do you know?’ This critical thinking develops gradually.
guide.watch: She correctly identifies fiction vs reality in books, TV, and play scenarios.
guide.see: Persistent confusion between reality and fantasy at 6 years may be worth discussing.

guide.try: Model empathy visibly — help others in front of her, discuss how characters in books might feel, respond empathetically to her own distress.
guide.watch: She spontaneously notices and responds to others’ emotional states.
guide.see: No empathy or emotional responsiveness to others by 6 years.

guide.try: Resist stepping in immediately during peer conflicts. Give her a chance to negotiate — then coach from the sideline if needed.
guide.watch: She uses words to propose compromises and reach agreement with peers.
guide.see: Exclusively aggressive or withdrawing responses to peer conflict by 6 years.

guide.try: Facilitate consistent contact with potential friends. Talk about what makes a good friend and model friendship in your own relationships.
guide.watch: A specific child she consistently seeks out, talks about, and enjoys spending time with.
guide.see: No peer relationships or social connections at all by 6 years.

relatedActivities.name: Games with real rules
relatedActivities.tagline: A card game with actual strategy is a very different beast from snakes and ladders.

guide.try: Daily 10-minute writing practice is more effective than occasional long sessions. Start with her name, then high-frequency words. Correct grip and posture from the start.
guide.watch: All alphabet letters formed legibly; simple words written independently.
guide.see: Cannot write her own name legibly by 6 years.

guide.try: Use objects, fingers, and everyday situations: ‘You have 4 grapes. I eat 2. How many are left?’ Concrete before abstract, always.
guide.watch: She solves simple addition and subtraction within 10 using objects or fingers reliably.
guide.see: No number sense or basic arithmetic by 6 years.
