guide.try: Talk through cause and effect in daily life and in stories: ‘Why did that happen? What could she have done differently?’ Open questions build this reasoning.
guide.watch: She explains ‘because’ correctly and predicts outcomes in familiar situations.
guide.see: No causal reasoning by 6 years.

guide.try: Sort the same set of objects in different ways: ‘Can we sort these another way?’ This flexibility of thinking is a precursor to mathematical and scientific reasoning.
guide.watch: She reclassifies the same objects by different criteria when asked.
guide.see: Very rigid, single-attribute thinking by 6 years.

relatedActivities.name: Everyday math
relatedActivities.tagline: Sharing six crackers between three people is real division, no worksheet needed.

activityIntro.headline: Getting ready for the next big step.
activityIntro.desc: The final year before formal schooling for most families is less about new skills appearing from nowhere and more about existing ones deepening — blending sounds into whole words, writing becoming legible rather than symbolic, counting turning into real early arithmetic, and independence extending into full daily routines. Games gain real rules and real strategy. Friendships gain real loyalty.
activityIntro.note: School readiness is not a single finish line every child crosses at the same pace. A child who isn't yet blending sounds fluently but is confident, curious, and socially secure is arguably better prepared than one further ahead academically but anxious about trying.

activities.name: Sound blending games
activities.tagline: "C... a... t" becoming "cat" out loud is the moment reading clicks.
activities.why: Blending — hearing separate sounds and merging them into a whole word — is the specific skill that turns letter knowledge into actual reading. It's a distinct cognitive step from simply knowing what sounds letters make, and for many children it's the exact moment reading shifts from abstract to real.
steps: Say a simple word slowly in separate sounds — "s... u... n" — and ask them to guess the whole word.
Start with short, familiar words (cat, sun, dog) before moving to less familiar ones.
Once blending feels solid, reverse it — say a whole word and ask them to break it into its sounds.
activities.parentNote: "This can be played entirely in the car with no materials at all. Some of the most useful pre-reading practice looks exactly like a silly guessing game, because that's exactly what it is."
watchFor: Blending simple three-sound words correctly — a strong reading-readiness sign
Attempting to sound out unfamiliar words independently — real decoding beginning
Significant, persistent difficulty blending even simple sounds by age 6 — worth raising with a teacher

activities.name: Writing their name
activities.tagline: Legible, not perfect, is the actual goal at this age.
activities.why: Writing one's own name reliably draws together letter knowledge, fine motor control, and sustained attention — three separate skills working in coordination. It's also frequently the first piece of writing a child feels genuine ownership and pride over, which matters for building a positive relationship with writing more broadly.
steps: Practice with a proper pencil grip on unlined paper before moving to lines — control comes before neatness.
Trace over a lightly written model of their name a few times before attempting it unaided.
Let them sign their own drawings, cards, and notes — real, purposeful writing sticks better than repeated practice sheets.
activities.parentNote: "A backwards letter here and there at five and six is developmentally typical, not a warning sign. Consistency and confidence matter far more right now than perfect formation."
watchFor: Writing their first name with reasonably consistent letters — solid progress
A comfortable, stable pencil grip — fine motor control maturing
Significant difficulty holding or controlling a pencil at all by age 6 — worth an occupational therapy check-in

activities.name: Retell in order
activities.tagline: Beginning, middle, end — the same shape as every essay they'll ever write.
activities.why: By this age, retelling can move beyond loosely recalling events toward genuinely sequenced storytelling with cause and effect — "the wolf blew the house down, so the pig ran to his brother's house." This causal sequencing is a direct foundation for reading comprehension and later academic writing.
steps: After a story, ask them to retell it using three simple markers: first, then, at the end.
Push gently for cause and effect: "why did that happen? What happened because of it?"
Try it with a real event from their day too, not just books — the same sequencing skill applies.
activities.parentNote: "Ask them to tell you about their day using 'first, then, at the end.' It turns a one-word answer into real practice at a skill they'll use for the rest of their schooling."
watchFor: Using "first, then, after that" unprompted — sequencing language internalised
Explaining why something happened, not just what happened — causal reasoning developing
Significant difficulty retelling even a very simple, familiar story by age 6 — worth mentioning to a teacher

activities.name: Everyday math
activities.tagline: Sharing six crackers between three people is real division, no worksheet needed.
activities.why: Simple addition and subtraction make far more sense to a five- or six-year-old when they're embedded in real, physical situations — sharing snacks, counting how many are left — than in abstract number sentences on paper. This concrete-before-abstract sequence is well established in how young children actually build number understanding.
steps: Use snacks, toys, or stairs for simple real-world addition and subtraction: "you have four, I'm giving you two more, how many now?"
Let them touch and move the objects to find the answer rather than guessing in their head.
Introduce fair sharing as a math problem: "six crackers, three people — how many each?"
activities.parentNote: "You are doing more real math at snack time than an entire page of sums could teach. Let the crackers do the work."
watchFor: Solving simple addition or subtraction with objects to help — solid concrete understanding
Attempting the same problems without objects sometimes — early mental math
No grasp of "more," "less," or simple combining by age 6 — worth mentioning to a teacher

activities.name: Morning routine, their way
activities.tagline: A visual checklist does more than a hundred verbal reminders.
activities.why: Managing a multi-step routine independently — wake, dress, brush teeth, eat, shoes — draws heavily on executive function: holding a sequence in mind and carrying it through without constant external direction. This is precisely the kind of self-management most schools expect to be emerging by this age.
steps: Build a simple picture-and-word checklist of the routine together, in order.
Let them check off or move a marker for each completed step, rather than you announcing what's next.
Step back and let natural consequences teach where safe — a forgotten water bottle is a better lesson than a reminder.
activities.parentNote: "Handing over the checklist can feel slower and messier than just doing it for them. It is. It's also exactly how independence gets built — one morning at a time."
watchFor: Completing most of the routine using the checklist alone — executive function developing
Noticing and fixing a skipped step themselves — self-monitoring emerging
Significant, ongoing difficulty following any multi-step routine by age 6 — worth mentioning to a teacher

activities.name: Games with real rules
activities.tagline: A card game with actual strategy is a very different beast from snakes and ladders.
activities.why: Games with a genuine strategic element — simple card games, memory-matching at scale, basic tactics games — ask a child to plan ahead, hold rules in mind, and adjust based on what an opponent does. This is a meaningfully more complex cognitive task than the pure-chance games that dominated earlier years, and most children are ready for it by now.
steps: Introduce one new game at a time, with full, unabbreviated rules — resist the urge to simplify prematurely.
Think aloud during your own turns so they can hear the reasoning behind a decision, not just the move itself.
Play the real game, including real losses — this is where the earlier work on losing gracefully pays off.
activities.parentNote: "By now, letting them win every time does them fewer favours than it used to. A genuine game, played straight, is a real vote of confidence in what they can handle."
watchFor: Planning a move ahead rather than reacting purely in the moment — strategic thinking emerging
Explaining their own reasoning for a move — metacognition developing
Persistent inability to follow even simple game rules by age 6 — worth a gentle conversation with a teacher
